next-env.d.ts.description: The `next-env.d.ts` file is a global TypeScript declaration automatically generated by Next.js to inform the compiler about the provided by the Next framework and its image handling module. It supplies ambient references for Next's API types (`next` and `/image-types/global`) so that components across the entire codebase can compile with correct typings for routing, server‑side rendering, and image optimization features without importing those types manually. This file is referenced implicitly by the TypeScript compiler during the build process; no runtime logic or state is executed from it. It is required for the app’s type safety but does not participate in any UI, workflow, or API logic, and typically other project files depend on it through the global type context. All components, hooks and utilities in the project implicitly use these references for type checking, enabling features such as `useRouter`, `Image` components, and environment variables tagged to `process.env`. The file follows Next.js documentation guidelines and remains untouched; its content is stable unless the Next.js version changes or a custom type configuration is added.

next.config.js.description: next.config supplies the primary Next.js configuration object exported as the default module; it specificallys external image loading through the Image component to two host: any subdomain of supabase.co over HTTPS lh3.googleusercontent.com, thereby allowing user avatars media stored on Supabase or Google user content to be securely. This configuration is consumed by every Next server and client build, affecting any page that and uses next/image to fetch external URLs. Ites no conditional logic or side effects on runtime code beyond the static config, but it is essential for preventing cache-busting or security warnings for remote images in the product’s UI layers such as profile pages or content feeds. No other files import or modify this config; it is referenced implicitly by the Next.js runtime when compiling and serving routes. The sole dependency is the Next.js framework itself, which reads this file automatically at start-up, and all components that call next/image rely on these remotePatterns to render properly.

postcss.config.mjs.description: The `postcss.config.mjs` file defines the PostCSS configuration for the build pipeline, exporting a default object that activates the Tailwind CSS plugin with its default options. This configuration is read by tools such as Vite or PostCSS during the asset bundling phase, allowing CSS authored in the project to be processed through Tailwind’s utility generation and purge logic before being emitted into the final bundle. There is no application‑level state, side effects, or API calls in this file—its sole purpose is to provide a declarative plugin list to the CSS compiler. It depends only on the `@postcss-load-config` type for static type checking, and any module that imports the build configuration (e.g., `vite.config.js

prisma/seed.ts.description: This Prisma seed script initializes the `system_config` table with a set of default configuration values used across the application, such as late‑threshold minutes and AI model identifiers. It imports `PrismaClient`, creates a singleton instance, and iterates over the `defaultConfigs` array, performing an `upsert` for each entry to ensure idempotent creation or update of settings keyed by `key`. During execution it logs the start of seeding, each successful upsert, and completion, providing console feedback for devs and CI pipelines. The script is intended to be run once during setup or database migration, and it closes the Prisma connection a `finally` block regardless of success, ensuring graceful shutdown. Dependencies include only `@prisma/client`; no other parts of the codebase call this module directly, but other will query `system_config` via the same PrismaClient once seeded. Key patterns include idempotent upserting of critical system defaults and controlled resource cleanup after asynchronous operations.

public/sw.js.description: The file implements a minimal Service Worker (SW) for DevRolin CRM that registers itself on install, immediately activates (skipWaiting, claim clients), and handles web‑push notifications. On receiving a push event it parses the payload as JSON or falls back to plain text, builds a notification with title, body, icon, and badge, and attaches a data URL (defaulting to “/dashboard”) and optional tag for deduplication. When the user clicks the notification the SW closes it, then tries to focus an existing tab on the same origin, navigating that client to the stored URL; if none are available it opens a new window. The SW deliberately disables caching, avoiding interference with authenticated app flows that rely on network requests to the main backend. It relies on the application having previously registered the SW and requested push permissions, and downstream code (e.g., the dashboard’s navigation logic) interprets the URL field to route to appropriate UI components while preserving origin safety. No external modules or state are imported; the file

src/app/(app)/accountant/page.tsx.description: The `AccountantPage` component renders the authenticated finance dashboard at `/accountant`, pulling a paginated list of account entries from the `/api/finance` endpoint, filtering by date, category, or createdBy; each column shows date, type badge (income/expense), category label, description, project title, amount in USD with optional original currency, receipt link, creator, and a Void button for non‑voided rows that opens a confirmation dialog. It also provides a “Create” button that lets the user open a modal to add either an income or an expense, presenting a form that captures category, date, amount, optional original amount/currency, description, optional project association, and an optional receipt file; the form’s submission posts to `/api/finance/income` or `/api/finance/expense`, uploads a receipt to `/api/storage/upload`, and updates the list on success

src/app/(app)/admin/hiring/page.tsx.description: AdminHiringPage renders the `/admin/hiring` admin route, fetching a list of pending hiring approvals from `/api/admin/hiring-approvals` on mount and re‑loading when the refresh button is clicked; it shows a loading spinner while the request is in flight and a “no requests” card when the list is empty. Each request card displays the role title, requestor, HR reviewer, deadline, and candidate count, with status badges and three approval chips (Manager, HR, Admin) whose background changes when the approval flag is true. An “Approve” or “Reject” button triggers a PATCH to `/api/admin/hiring-approvals/<id>` that updates the `adminApproved` flag; the UI updates optimistic state, removes the card if approved, and shows success or error toasts using react‑hot‑toast. The component maintains local state for the request list, a loading flag, and the ID of a request currently being approved to disable buttons and show a spinner. It relies on the lucide icons for visual cues and Tailwind‑CSS utility classes, and it expects the calling context to provide authentication so that only an admin can view/act on this page. The page’s side effects are single‑fetch init, conditional rendering based on state, and side‑effectful toast notifications. The file is imported by Next.js as a client

When mounted it fetches the users via an API call, then lets the viewer filter them and pick one to view details: role, work mode, work hours, and active sessions. src/app/(app)/attendance/page.tsx.description: The `AttendancePage` component renders the /attendance route for authenticated employees, pulling the current user’s role, the day‑slice of attendance data and the month‑wide log via back‑end queries (GET /attendance?date=… and GET /attendance/history). It displays the user avatar and name, shows whether the employee is currently checked‑in via a badge and togg

src/app/(app)/dashboard/page.tsx.description: The `DashboardPage` component implements the `/dashboard` page in a Next.js 13+ application, enforcing server‑side authentication by reading anaccess_token` cookie, verifying it, and redirect to `/login` if missing or invalid. After verifying the JWT payload, it ensures the role enum is valid, fetches both the user profile and aggregated dashboard statistics in parallel, and aborts the request with a redirect if the profile fails. It passes the user’s name and role formatted for display to `DashboardHeader` and `DashboardClientStrip`, then renders `StatsRow` with key metrics. If chart data is present it renders a visualisation through `DashboardCharts`. The final layout arranges `RecentActivity` and role‑specific `RoleWidgets` in a responsive grid. This file depends on the Prisma client, token verification, the `fetchDashboardData` service, and several presentational components in `components/dashboard`, and it is the endpoint for authenticating and presenting the user’s dashboard UI. The page is marked `dynamic="force-dynamic"` to ensure it is re‑rendered on every request, keeping the displayed data up‑to‑date.

src/app/(app)/error.tsx.description: This file defines a client‑side component that handles rendering a generic error UI for use as a Next.js error boundary or‑all error page; it receives an `error object and a `reset` callback from Next.js. The component detects whether the error message contains “” or “prisma” to tailor the, showing a “Database Unavailable” heading with guidance about Supabase pauses, otherwise a generic “Something went” message with the error text or a fallback It displays a diagnostic icon from lucide-react, a message block, and a “Try again” button that invokes the supplied `reset` function to re‑attempt the failed request. No external state or API calls are performed; the logic is purely presentational and conditional. It relies only on React and lucide-react, and is exported as the default component expected by Next.js in `app/error.tsx`. The component is used by the framework when query failures or server‑side errors occur, replacing the page UI until the reset succeeds.

It renders the request’s metadata, status tabs, and a dynamically populated list of candidate applications, pulling candidate data, interview schedules, and offering actions such as

src/app/(app)/hr/employees/page.tsx.description: This page renders the `/hr/employees` Next.js route, displaying a of active employee cards with avatar, contact, role, work mode, salary, and performance metrics; it fetches data from the `/api/hr/employees` endpoint on mount and populates local `employees` state. A search input filters employees by name, email, or role, updating `searchQuery` and re‐rendering `filteredEmployees`. Each card offers a button that opens a modal pre‑filled with the employee’s current salary and allows a proposed salary to be submitted via a POST to `/api/hr/employees/{id}/salary-request`; the response may auto‑approve the request, in which case the local state is updated and a success toast shown, otherwise a request-to-admins message is displayed. The component conditionally renders a loading indicator, an empty‑search banner with a `UserCheck` icon, and a custom `CheckCircle` icon for task metrics because it is not imported from Lucide. All interactions use `react-hot-toast` for user feedback, while `useState` tracks loading, search, modal, form, and submitting status. The file depends on `UserAvatar` for profile images and uses Dapper‑styled Tailwind classes; its parent layout is presumably a protected HR shell that ensures authentication before rendering.

src/app/(app)/hr/layout.tsx.description: `HrLayout` is a client‑side layout component used for every route in the `/hr` segment of the application; it wraps the section’s pages with an effect that temporarily overrides the global sidebar navigation. On mount it calls `getHrSidebarItems()` from the sidebar config to obtain HR‑specific sidebar entries and passes them to `setOverride` from the `useSidebarOverride` context provided by `ClientLayout`. This ensures that while any HR route is active the sidebar reflects only HR‑relevant links, and when the component unmounts it restores the default sidebar by setting the override to `null`. The component itself renders nothing but passes through its `children`, making it a thin wrapper that manipulates shared layout state. It relies on the `

src/app/(app)/hr/page.tsx.description: This file implements the `/hr` dashboard for authenticated users, fetching initial data from the `/api/hr` endpoints and rendering a hiring‑request pipeline, stats charts, and attendance alerts. On mount it performs parallel `fetch` calls to `/api/hr/requests`, `/api/hr/attendance-alerts`, and `/api/hr/stats`, populating `requests`, `alerts`, and `stats` states, and shows a spinner while loading. The pipeline summary uses request counts by status, while `HrDashboardCharts` consumes `stats` for visual charts; these components are imported from `@/components/dashboard/HrDashboardCharts`. Users can create a new hiring request via a modal that posts to `/api/hr/requests`; upon success it redirects to the request detail page using `router.push`. Clicking a request row or clicking the “New Request” button updates navigation; the component also exposes toast notifications with custom styles for success or error. The page relies on client‑side React state, Next.js navigation, `lucide‑react` icons, and `react-hot-toast`; it is the default export of `src/app/(app)/hr/page.tsx` and is rendered by Next.js when the `/hr` route is accessed.

src/app/(app)/meetings/[id]/page.tsx.description: The `MeetingJoinPage` component renders the client‑side meeting view for the dynamic route `/meetings/[id]`. On mount it extracts the `id` param with `useParams`, calls the protected API `/api/meetings/${meetingId}/join-token` with `no-store` cache to obtain a `MeetingJoinData` payload containing LiveKit room details, a signed token, and participant metadata; it sets `meeting`, `loading`, and `error` state accordingly while guarding against race conditions with a `cancelled` flag. If the call fails or the meeting is not found, it displays an error card with a button to redirect to `/chat`;

src/app/(app)/notifications/page.tsx.description: The NotificationsPage component renders the authenticated `/notifications` route, fetching up to 100 notifications from `/api/notifications` on mount. It groups the result by date label, stores these groups in local state, and pre‑captures ids of unread notifications to apply a temporary visual highlight before sending a `POST` to `/api/notifications/mark-all-read`. The page shows a loading spinner while data is loaded, a “Mark all read” button when any notifications exist, or a friendly empty state if none are present. Each group displays a date divider and iterates over its notifications, rendering an icon (from `@/lib/notification-icons`), a title, parsed body, and a relative timestamp using dayjs’ `relativeTime`. Clickable notifications push to their `linkUrl` via `router.push`. The component depends on dayjs, icon/color mappings, a body formatting helper, and Prisma’s `NotificationType` for typing; it

src/app/(app)/profile/page.tsx.description: ProfilePage renders the authenticated user’s profile view under the `/profile` route, displaying personal data, avatar, salary (for non‑CLIENT roles), and a list of contracts. It loads the profile and contract data from the backend on mount, showing loading spinners while `fetchingProfile` or `fetchingContracts` are true, and handles empty states for the contract list. Users can edit fields via the `InlineEditField` component wired to the `useInlineEdit` hook, which posts updated values back to the server and gives toast feedback; the avatar is updated through a modal that uses `react-image-crop` to crop an uploaded image and then uploads the result to the server, toggling `uploading` state during the request. Conditional UI shows “Sign” buttons for pending contracts, invoking `handleSign` which calls the sign‑contract API and refreshes the list. The component depends on UI primitives such as `UserAvatar`, `ContractVersionList`, and icons from Lucide, and it leverages a modal dialog

src/app/(app)/projects/[id]/ai/page.tsx.description: The `ProjectAIPage` component renders the AI‑assistant panel for a specific project at the route `/projects/[id]/ai`; it fetches the conversation history from the backend (`/api/projects/${id}/ai-chat`) on mount, shows a loading spinner until the data arrives, and scrolls to the bottom whenever the message list changes. Users type in a resizable textarea that sends a message on Enter (Shift‑Enter keeps the line), triggering an optimistic UI update, a POST to the same API with the question, and a subsequent GET to refresh the full history so message IDs stay consistent; errors are reported with `react-hot-toast`. Each message is displayed with conditional styling: user messages align right with a primary background, while assistant messages use `AiMarkdown` to render Markdown. The component depends on `AiMarkdown` for rendering assistant responses and on `lucide-react` icons for UI elements. It is consumed by the project detail layout and expects valid authentication to be handled by the API layer; on success the refreshed history is stored in component state (`messages`) and drives the UI rendering. src/app/(app)/projects/[id]/chat/page.ts.description: The ProjectChatPage component is a client‑side page rendering the chat view for a specific project (route `/projects/[id]/chat`). It fetches a list of chat rooms and the current user via `/api/chat/rooms?projectId=…` and `/api/users/me`, tracks loading, and displays a toast on fetch failure. It then joins all rooms through a `/chat` socket namespace and updates the local room list in real‑time when `new_message` events arrive, bumping unread counts unless the room is currently selected. Role‑based routing logic chooses which UI layout to show: clients see only the `project_client_manager` room as a full‑screen chat, internal contributors (non‑client, non‑manager) see only the `project_team_group` room, and managers or other contributors see a two‑pane layout with a sidebar listing “manager rooms” (team or client‑manager) where each entry shows avatars, unread badge, last message preview, and allows selection; selecting a room clears its unread count and, on mobile, collapses the list. The sidebar auto‑selects

src/app/(app)/projects/[id]/documents/pa.description: The `DocumentsPage` component renders the document management interface for a single project, including a tree view of documents, a block‑note editor that's wrapped with a collaboration token, and controls for sharing, exporting, printing, and deleting the currently selected document. It shows a toggle that enables or disables public sharing, and when shared it displays a read‑only link that can be copied to the clipboard with a toast confirmation, using the `togglePublicSharing` API call. Export options are offered through a dropdown with actions that call `printDoc`, `downloadMarkdown`, or `copyToClipboard`, all of which trigger the respective download or clipboard side‑effects. Only managers (not the system user) and non‑requirements documents can be deleted via the `deleteDoc` mutation, with visual feedback provided by an error‑styled button. The editor area renders `DocumentEditor` with props including `readOnly={!canEdit}` and initial content from the selected doc; while the collaboration token loads, a

src/app/(app)/projects/[id]/layout.tsx.description: ProjectLayout is a client‑side layout component for the `/projects/[id]` that fetches the project title and configures a sidebar based on the authenticated user role; it uses `useParams` and `usePathname` to build breadcrumb navigation and the current sub‑section label (Dashboard, Chat, Meetings, etc.). It calls the API `/api/projects/[id]` to load the project title, and `/api/projectsid]/questions` to compute an unanswered‑questions badge, dispatching `sidebar-badge` events on load and every 30 s, clearing the badge on unmount. The component leverages a `useSidebarOverride` hook from ClientLayout to inject a custom sidebar navigation generated by `getProjectSidebarItems`, ensuring role‑based access; it also listens to the `useUser` hook for role changes. Breadcrumb links route to `/projects` and the project dashboard, with the project link highlighted while the sub‑section label is omitted on the dashboard. This layout sits just inside the `/projects/[id]` group, wrapping page children, and is consumed by all nested sub‑routes; other components may listen for the badge events to update the sidebar. The implementation pattern uses cleanup effects to cancel stale async requests and unregister overrides, maintaining correct state across navigation.

src/app/(app)/projects/[id]/meetings/pag.description: The `ProjectMeetingsPage` component renders a client‑side page under `/projects/[id]/meetings` that lets authenticated users view, schedule, and upload recordings of video meetings for a specific project. It pulls the project ID via the route parameter, then uses local state to manage two modals: a two‑step “Create Client Meeting” form that collects title, optional scheduled date/time, and selected team members, and an “Upload Recording” dialog that uploads a video file to the backend. When the user submits the meeting form it calls the API to create a meeting record, optionally scheduling it, and shows a toast on success; the upload dialog submits a multipart/form‑data request to attach a recording to an existing meeting. The component also conditionally renders a full‑screen LiveKit

src/app/(app)/projects/[id]/questions/pa.description: The page component renders the questions panel for a specific project under the dynamic route `/projects/[id]/questions`; it fetches the current user, project details, questions list, milestones, and the user’s role via server‑side props and stores them in local state. It displays “Needed to Start” questions first, then groups remaining questions by each milestone order, each rendered by a `QuestionCard` that shows the question text, approval status, contributor, and the latest answer with optional history toggling. Non‑client users see an “Add Question” button that opens a form to submit a new question, which creates an optimistic UI update and sends a POST to the API; managers can edit or delete questions, while clients can answer or update

The UI divides tasks into “Current” (not DONE/CANCELLED) and “Past” (“DONE” or “CANCELLED”), displaying status icons, labels, relative creation time and assignee avatars, and each row links to the task detail via `router.push`. When the user has a manager or admin role, a second fetch retrieves pending client requests from `/api/projects/client-requests`, displaying them in a modal where items can be ignored via a PATCH request or expanded to pre‑populate a new project form. src/app/(app)/settings/page.tsx.description: This file implements the authenticated Settings responsible for changing user preferences. It defines a SettingsContent component that fetches the signed‑in user/api/users/me`) and renders four panels—, Appearance, Currency, Notification, and Google account integration—selected via local state. Each panel contains form controls that mutate local state and use an async `saveSetting` helper to PATCH `/api/users/me/settings` while showing toast; notification toggles explicitly request `/api/users/me/settings/notifications` and optimistically update the UI. The Google section conditionally displays a “Connect” or “Disconnect” button that triggers `/api/auth/google/init?flow=connect` or `/api/auth/google/disconnect`, updates the local `user.isGoogleConnected` flag, and redirects to the home page on success. The page relies on other shared components (e.g., ThemeToggle, NavGroup) and the ThemeProvider context, and is consumed by the app router under the protected `(app)` group, providing a fully client‑side experience for authenticated settings management.

src/app/(app)/tasks/[id]/page.tsx.description: This client‑side page renders the detailed view for a single task at the route `/tasks/[id]`. It fetches the task, its chat‑room ID and the current user in parallel; if the task belongs to a project it redirects to the project's task list. The UI shows a two‑column desktop layout with a details panel (title, status dropdown, creation meta, assignees, and an auto‑saving StandaloneEditor for the description) and a chat panel powered by ChatRoom, with a modal for meeting recordings. State tracks the task object, status changes, description changes (auto‑save timer), modal visibility, and meeting‑starting status; unfinished data turns to loading spinners. Status changes trigger a `/api/tasks/:id` PATCH, description changes invoke the same API with debounce, and starting a meeting posts to `/api/meetings/start`, opening a new tab with the meeting URL. src/app/(app)/tasks/page.tsx.description: The file implements the authenticated “Tasks” sidebar page that fetches all tasks, projects, and users on mount, then renders a toggleable list or kanban view with tabs for “All”, “General”, and “Project” filtered tasks. It manages local state for loading, task data, view mode, tab, a task‑detail modal id, a task‑creation modal and its form fields, and an optimistic status update handler that patches `/api/tasks/:id` before reverting on failure. New tasks are created via `/api/tasks` POST, re‑adding the result to the local task array and resetting the form, with toast notifications styled by `TOAST_STYLE`. Presence information for assignees is provided by the `usePresence` hook from `PresenceProvider` and passed to each `TaskCardComponent`. The component renders a centered, warning‑styled UI with a ShieldAlert icon, a headline “Access denied,” a brief explanatory paragraph, and a primary button linking back to the dashboard. It imports only Next.js Link for navigation and Lucide’s ShieldAlert icon, with no additional state, hooks, or network calls. The page is intended to be displayed when auth middleware deems the user lacking permission for a protected resource, acting as a fallback UI for denied access. It does not trigger side effects or modify global state and simply presents static content. src/app/(app)/workspaces/[id]/page.tsx.description: WorkspaceBoardPage is a client‑side React page that renders a workspace dashboard for a specific workspace ID passed in the URL; it fetches the workspace and current user from `/api/workspaces/[id]` and `/api/auth/me`, displaying a loading spinner or error toast as needed. Once data is present, it shows a header with icon, name, description, member count, and real‑time presence via PresenceProvider, and offers actions to open a recordings modal that lists talks from `/api/meetings/workspace/{id}` or to start a new meeting through `/api/meetings/create`. The page lists tasks in three sections—active, in‑review, and archived—rendering each via WorkspacePostCard and opening WorkspaceTaskModal on click for editing, updating, or deleting tasks, with optimistic UI updates and error handling. It also includes a “New Post” modal that collects a title, creates a task using `/api/tasks/create`, and provides loading feedback, plus a Members modal that lets role‑based managers search for users (`/api/users/search`) and add or remove members, leveraging a private presence map to indicate online status and disabling

src/app/(app)/workspaces/page.tsx.description: The WorkspacesPage component renders the authenticated dashboard for managing social‑media workspaces and is the page at `/workspaces`; it fetches the list of workspaces and the current user from `/api/workspaces` and `/api/auth/me` on mount, showing a loading spinner until data arrives. Once loaded, it displays a card grid of workspaces with an icon per workspace type, task count, and avatar initials for members, and users in the ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER roles can open a modal to create a new workspace; the modal collects name, type, and optional description, submitting a POST to `/api/workspaces` and navigating to the new workspace on success. The component uses `useRouter` for navigation, `toast` for success/error feedback, and `lucide-react` icons for type badges and the modal UI. Conditional rendering hides member avatars beyond four and shows a "+N" token when necessary, and the create button is disabled until a non‑empty name is entered or a create operation is pending. This component depends only on core React, Next.js client routing, and UI icon/notification libraries; it is consumed by any route that needs to list or create social‑media boards

src/app/(auth)/invite/[token]/page.tsx.description: The `/invite/[token]/page.tsx` component is a client‑side React page that renders on the route `/invite/:token` and handles the full accept‑invite workflow for DevRolin. Upon mounting it fetches invite metadata from the backend endpoint `/api/invite/:token`; while the request is in flight it shows a skeleton card, on error it shows an error card with a link to `/login`, and on success it displays the invitation details including inviter name, email and role. The initial input field for the user’s name is pre‑filled from the invitation email and validated against a 2‑50 character letter/space/hyphen regex; if invalid a helper text appears. Submitting the form posts `{token, name}` to `/api/invite/accept`; a successful response triggers a success card that auto‑redirects to `/login` after 2 seconds, while failures update the generic error state. The component leverages Next.js `useRouter` for navigation, `useParams` for token extraction, and local React state for loading, error

src/app/(auth)/layout.tsx.description: The `AuthLayout` in `src/app/(auth)/layout.tsx` is the root layout for all authenticated‐section routes (e.g., login, register, forgot password) in a Next.js app, wrapping those pages with a global theme provider and a theme toggle button. It imports `ThemeProvider` from the project’s providers and `ThemeToggle` for user‑controlled light/dark mode switching. The component receives `children`, which are the specific auth page components, and renders them inside a full‑height div styled with Tailwind to provide a base background (`bg-base‑100`). The layout injects the `ThemeProvider` context so any descendant can consume the current theme state, and mounts `ThemeToggle` at the root of the auth section for convenient UI toggling. No network or business logic is executed—significant state is limited to the theme context—making it a lightweight structural wrapper. It resides under the Next.js `app/(auth)` “layout” folder, so the file automatically applies to every route inside that group;

src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx.description: This client‑side page renders the /login view inside the (auth) group, showing a centered card with a Zap icon, an email/password form, a “Forgot password?” link, and a Google login button; it uses a <Suspense> skeleton while the search parameters resolve. An effect on component mount calls /api

src/app/(auth)/otp/page.tsx.description: The `OtpPage` component renders the six‑digit verification page under the `/forgot-password` auth flow, showing a skeleton loader until a mounting side‑effect confirms a pending OTP via `/api/auth/otp/check` and redirects to `/forgot-password` if the check fails. It manages a `otp` array state, auto‑focusing the next input, handling backspace navigation, and supports pasting a full six‑digit code into the first field, updating focus accordingly. On submit it POSTs the joined code to `/api/auth/verify-otp`, displays server‑generated errors, triggers a shaking animation for 400/429 responses, and redirects to `/reset-password` upon success. The resend button invokes `/api/auth/forgot-password`, starts a 60‑second cooldown timer, disables the button during cooldown, and shows a countdown. All API calls use `fetch`, with error handling that covers network failures, rate‑limits, and unauthorized state. The component relies on Next's `useRouter` for navigation

src/app/(auth)/reset-password/page.tsx.description: The file exports a client‑side Next.js page that renders the “reset‑password” step of the authentication flow under the protected “(auth)” route. On mount it performs a GET to `/api/auth/reset/check`; if the server responds with a non‑OK status it redirects the user back to `/forgot-password`, ensuring the token is still valid before displaying the form. The UI consists of a centered card with a back button, a branding icon, a header, an error alert, and a form with two password inputs that support show/hide toggles and display a “PasswordStrength” component diagramming length, case, numeric, and special‑character requirements, and a live match‑check. Submission posts JSON to `/api/auth/reset-password`; the component interprets 429, 401, and 400 responses to show specific messages or redirect, and on success navigates to `/dashboard`. State variables track the passwords, visibility flags, loading, error message, and a `mounted` flag that avoids flashing the UI before the validation check. The component relies only on React hooks, Next’s router, Link, and Lucide icons; it is used by the routing system as a page and by the API routes it calls. This pattern enforces client‑side validation, progressive disclosure, and secure redirect flow in the overall authentication flow.

src/app/(public)/careers/[slug]/page.tsx.description: The CareersPage component serves the public job‑listing route `(public)/careers/[slug]`; it fetches a Job object from `/api/public/careers/${slug}` via `useEffect`, rendering a loader while pending, a not‑found UI if the fetch fails, or the job details and application form when data is present. The form collects personal data (name, email, optional phone), an optional PDF CV with size and MIME validation, and dynamic application questions driven by the job’s `questions` array (handling text, long text, number, date, email, URL, boolean via radio pairs, file uploads), storing answers in a `Record` state that maps question IDs to values or files. On submit it builds a `FormData` bundle (including the hCaptcha token) and posts to `/api/public/careers/${slug}/apply`, handling success, network or server errors by resetting the captcha and showing an alert. The component disables the submit button until the captcha resolves, toggles sub‑mit state, and displays a success pane when the application is accepted. It relies on `next/navigation`’s `useParams`,

src/app/(public)/join/meeting/[id]/page..description: This client‑side page renders the guest join flow for a meeting at `/join/meeting/[id]`. It extracts the `id` parameter and an optional `name` query, then dynamically imports the `LiveKitMeeting` component to avoid SSR. On mount it fetches a guest join‑token

src/app/(public)/layout.tsx.description: PublicLayout serves as the root layout component for the “(public)” route segment in the Next.js app router, wrapping all public pages without adding any additional markup or context—its sole purpose is to propagate the `children` from nested routes. It exports a default function that receives a React node and returns that node in a fragment, effectively making the public routes overlay a bare‑bones shell. This layout is intentionally minimal; it does not manipulate state, trigger side effects, or perform API calls. In the overall codebase it is referenced by Next.js when rendering paths under the `/app/(public)` directory, overriding the global layout only for public-facing pages while leaving authenticated or admin sections to use their own layouts. Because it renders nothing but `children`, it introduces no visual changes, but it provides a clear separation between public and protected route scopes, making future customization (e.g., adding a public header or theme context) straightforward. No dependencies or nested providers are required, so other components that import `PublicLayout` will simply receive the unmodified children passed down from the router.

src/app/(super-admin)/control/page.tsx.description: The page implements the Super‑Admin “Control” dashboard at `/super-admin/control`, rendering a vertical tab‑style navigator that switches between six stateless sections: System Config, Audit Logs, Sessions, Blacklist, Push Test, and DB Stats. State is kept locally for the active tab and each sub‑section manages its own data through `useEffect`‑driven `fetch` calls to server endpoints such as `/api/authorization`, `/api/audit-logs`, `/api/refresh-sessions`, `/api/blacklist`, `/api/super-admin/push-test`, and `/api/super-admin/db-stats`; loading spinners are shown while awaiting responses. The Push Test panel includes a debounced search that queries `/api/users?q=` for an email/name

src/app/(super-admin)/layout.tsx.description: The SuperAdminLayout is a server‑side layout in Next.js

src/app/api/accountant/entries/[id]/rout.description: The `route.ts` file implements the PATCH endpoint for the dynamic Next.js route `/api/accountant/entries/[id]`, enabling privileged users (ADMIN or ACCOUNTANT) to modify or void a specific accounting entry by ID. It first authenticates the request using `x-user-id` and role headers, rejecting unauthenticated or unterminated roles with a 401 or 403 response via the helper `forbidden`. The JSON body is validated against a Zod schema accommodating optional boolean `isVoid`, optional string `voidReason`, and optional string `description` with length constraints. After fetching the target `accountEntry` by Prisma, the handler ensures the entry exists and is not already voided, returning a 404 or 409 error if violated. The update applies only the fields provided in the request, leveraging dynamic spreads, and returns the updated `id`, `isVoid`, `voidReason`, and `description`. An audit log records the user, action type, and changed fields via `logAction`. This module relies on the shared `prisma` client, a generic `apiHandler` wrapper that handles errors and structure, and the `audit` logger; it is consumed by the

src/app/api/accountant/entries/route.ts.description: This file implements the backend API for accountant entry management in the accountant section, exposing a forced‑dynamic Next API route at `/api/accountant/entries`. The GET handler authenticates via `x-user-id` and `x-user-role`, allows only ADMIN or ACCOUNTANT roles, parses query parameters (`type`, `category`, `projectId`, date range, pagination) and returns a paginated list of non‑void entries with related project and creator data, adding a summary of total income, expense and profit computed with Prisma’s `groupBy`. The POST handler similarly authorizes the user and supports both JSON and multipart form submissions to support receipt uploads; it validates the body with Zod, uploads receipt images to Supabase storage if provided, creates a new `accountEntry` record with Prisma, and writes an audit log via `logAction`. It relies on the shared `prisma` instance, schema validation (`entrySchema`), generic `apiHandler` error handling, `forbidden` helper, and Supabase file

src/app/api/admin/hiring-approvals/[id]/.description: This file implements the PATCH endpoint /api/admin/hiring-approvals/[id] for approving or rejecting a hiring request. It enforces that only users whose “x-user-role” header is ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER can access the route, otherwise it returns a 403 Forbidden; the user ID is read from the "x-user-id" header for audit logging. The handler retrieves the targeted request by ID and validates the request payload with Zod to ensure an adminApproved boolean is supplied. It then updates the hiringRequest record: if adminApproved is true the field is set, otherwise the status is reset to DRAFT, and it preserves other approval flags. After the update, it logs an audit action (ADMIN_APPROVE_HIRING or ADMIN_REJECT_HIRING) with the admin ID, request type, and ID. Finally, it responds with the updated record including all approval flags and status; the endpoint relies on the Prisma client and audit helper and is consumed by the admin UI where hiring approvals are managed.

src/app/api/admin/hiring-approvals/route.description: This route exposes the `/api/admin/hiring-approvals` endpoint and forces dynamic rendering so it always runs server‑side. It authorizes the request by checking the `x-user-role` header against the `ADMIN_ROLES` list, returning a 403 JSON error if the user isn’t an ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER. If authorized, it queries Prisma for all hiring requests that are unfinished (adminApproved false, status not DRAFT), ordering them by creation time and selecting core fields, nested relation counts, and related user and HR data. The response JSON contains a `data` array of these request objects, each including identifiers, role titles, status flags, timestamps, deadline, and relationship counts for candidates. PATCH logic for individual request approval is delegated to a sibling `[id]` router file. The route depends on Next.js server utilities, the `prisma` client, the `zod` schema library (unused but imported), and audit logging (`logAction` is currently not invoked but available for future side‑effects). Other parts of the system, such as the admin dashboard UI and hiring request CRUD pages, invoke this endpoint to populate approval tables and drive state updates.

src/app/api/admin/users/[id]/reset-passw.description: POST /api/admin/users/[id]/reset-password is a force‑dynamic Next.js API route that lets an authorized admin trigger a password reset for a specific user ID; it begins by calling requireUserManagement to enforce the admin‑only guard, extracting the acting admin’s ID and role from request headers. The handler fetches the target user via Prisma, refusing to reset a SUPER_ADMIN password unless the caller is also a SUPER_ADMIN, and if the user is missing it returns a 404. Upon validation it generates a 6‑digit OTP, hashes it with bcrypt, and stores a purpose‑tagged record in the database with a 10‑minute expiry. A Resend email is dispatched to the user’s address, embedding the OTP and a link that passes the user’s email and purpose for reconciliation on the front‑end. The function logs the admin action with logAction for audit purposes and responds with a JSON payload indicating that the email was sent. This route is

src/app/api/admin/users/[id]/route.ts.description: The `route.ts` file implements the dynamic admin API endpoints for viewing and updating a user identified by the URL segment `[id]`. It exports a `GET` handler that retrieves the user with selected fields and any currently active sessions (those whose `expiresAt` is in the future), ordering sessions by most recent use, and a `PATCH` handler that validates incoming request payload with a Zod schema (allowing optional updates to name, role, work mode, stated role, active status, and work hour strings matching a `HH:MM` format). Both handlers employ the `requireUserManagement` guard to enforce that the caller possesses admin‑level permissions; on failure they return the guard’s response. The `PATCH` route deliberately blocks modifications to `SUPER_ADMIN` users, returning a 404 for those accounts, and logs the action via `logAction`. The endpoints rely on Prisma client for data access and rely on the audit logger and auth guard modules, and are consumed by the admin dashboard UI route `/admin/users/[id]`, which displays the user details and session list and provides a form that submits to these PATCH and GET routes.

src/app/api/admin/users/[id]/sessions/ro.description: This file implements the DELETE API for `//admin/users/[id]/sessions`, enabling an admin to invalidate a single session (`sessionId`) or all sessions for a target user (`all: true`). It first calls `requireUserManagement` to enforce admin‑only access, then retrieves the target user’s role and prohibits session deletion if the target is a `SUPER_ADMIN` and the actor is not a super admin. After validating the request body with Zod (union of `{sessionId}` or `{all:true}`), it performs the deletion with Prisma’s `deleteMany` and logs the action via `logAction`, recording the admin ID, action type, and any relevant payload (session ID or user ID and count). The route is marked `dynamic="force-dynamic"` to ensure each request re‑validates state. The module depends on Prisma for data access, an audit logger, and an admin‑user‑management guard, and its responses are consumed by admin dashboards that allow session revocation.

src/app/api/admin/users/create-direct/ro.description: This route handles POST /​api​/admin​/users​/create‑direct, validating incoming JSON with Zod, ensuring the requester passes `requireUserManagement`, and then checking that the provided email is not already in use. It generates a 16‑character random password, hashes it with bcrypt, and atomically creates a user record together with a default notificationPreference via a Prisma transaction. After persisting the user, it sends a welcome email containing the plain‑text password and login link using the Resend API, and logs the admin action through `logAction` for audit purposes. The handler relies on environment variables for Resend credentials, the sender email, and the app URL, and returns a 201 with the new user's basic data. This file is part of the admin panel backend, being called when an admin creates a user directly from the admin UI, and it orchestrates DB persistence, email notification, and audit logging before responding.

src/app/api/admin/users/route.ts.description: The `/api/admin/users` endpoint is a server‑side Next.js route that returns a list of non‑super‑admin users along with a count of their sessions. It first invokes `requireUserManagement` to ensure the request originates from an authorized admin; if the guard fails, the short‑circuit response is returned immediately. The handler reads an optional `q` query parameter from the request URL and, if present, filters the Prisma `user` query to include only rows whose `name` or `email` contain the search term (case‑insensitive) while still excluding users with the `SUPER_ADMIN` role. Query results are ordered by `createdAt` descending and slimmed to only the id, name, email, role, workMode, statedRole, isActive, profilePicUrl, createdAt, and a sub‑select counting related `sessions`. The JSON response contains a `data` property wrapping the array of user objects, making the endpoint suitable for consuming by an admin‑users table in the UI. This file depends on the Prisma client and the user‑management guard, while other parts of the code—such as the admin users page—request this route to populate its list view. The pattern of force‑dynamic routing and role‑based guarding is consistent with other admin API routes in the repository.

src/app/api/ai/chat/route.ts.description: The `/api/ai/chat` POST endpoint authenticates a request via the `x-user-id` and `x-user-role` headers, rejecting unauthenticated callers and users with the “CLIENT” role. It validates a zod schema for an array of role‑specified messages and optional `projectId`, `taskId`, `workspaceId`, and `newChat` flags. When context identifiers are present it calls `gatherContext` to fetch project titles, milestones, documents, task details, and workspace info from Prisma, truncating the aggregate to ~3000 characters. It then composes

src/app/api/ai/document/route.ts.description: The file implements the POST `/api/ai/document` endpoint for the project's AI‑powered document editing feature. It first enforces authentication by requiring an `x-user-id` header, otherwise it returns a 401 response. A Zod schema validates the incoming JSON, expecting a `prompt`, optional `context`, and a `type` with a `draft` default. Depending on the `type`, a predefined system prompt is selected to guide the LLM (draft, improve, summarize

src/app/api/ai/generate-milestones/route.description: The route.ts file implements a Next.js API endpoint (POST at /api/ai/generate-milestones) that generates a project milestone plan by invoking an AI model. It first validates the user role from the “x‑user‑role” header, allowing only ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER and rejecting others with a 403 via forbidden(). The request body is parsed against a Zod schema requiring a title, description, and optionally an array of existing milestone drafts. These drafts are formatted into an instruction string which is passed to callAIJson along with a user role message, prompting a Groq LLM to return an array of milestone objects (title/content) in strict JSON. If the AI response fails to produce a valid array, the handler returns a 502 error; otherwise it replies with the milestones wrapped in a data field. The endpoint relies on @/lib/api-handler for standardized error handling and @/lib/ai for the AI call, and it is likely invoked by a

src/app/api/ai/generate-questions/route..description: The POST endpoint at `/api/ai/generate-questions` is a server‑side route that verifies the caller’s role via the `x‑user‑role` header, allowing only “ADMIN” or “PROJECT_MANAGER” users; otherwise it returns a 403 by invoking `forbidden()`. It parses the request

src/app/api/ai/hr-notes/route.ts.description: The file implements a server‑side POST handler at `/api/ai/hr-notes` that generates concise internal HR evaluation notes for a candidate. It first authenticates the requester via an `x-user-id` header and authorizes only users whose `x-user-role` is one of `ADMIN`, `PROJECT_MANAGER`, or `HR`, returning 401 or 403 as appropriate. The handler parses a JSON payload containing `candidateId`, retrieves the candidate record and related request and answer data from Prisma, and constructs a structured prompt that includes the role applied for, candidate details, and interview answers. It then calls the `callAI` helper with a system prompt positioning the model as an HR professional, limiting output to 500 tokens and a 0.5 temperature, to produce the notes. The response is a JSON object `{data: {notes}}`. The route depends on `@/lib/prisma`, `@/lib/api-handler`, and `@/lib/ai`, and is consumed by the HR UI workflow that submits this POST request when an HR user wants to generate or refresh candidate notes for review.

src/app/api/ai/hr-recommend-candidates/r.description: The `/api/ai/hr-recommend-candidates` POST authenticates a user via an `x‑user‑id` header, checks that the role is one of ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, or HR, then parses a JSON body containing a `requestId`. It queries Prisma the hiring request with that ID, pulling the position title, public description, ordered questions, and any non‑hired/rejected candidates’ answers. If no candidates remain, it immediately returns an empty array; otherwise it constructs a prompt asking an LLM to score each candidate from 1‑10 and return a JSON array of `{candidateId, name, score, reasoning}`. The `callAIJson` helper invokes the AI service with low temperature and provides a JSON example; if the call fails a 500 error is returned. Candidates with scores ≥ 7 are then marked `isAiRecommended: true` in the DB via `updateMany`. Finally, the rankings array is sent back to the caller. This route is used by HR UI screens that trigger AI candidate evaluation, depends on `@/lib/api-handler`, `@/lib/ai`, and `@/lib/prisma`, and other parts of the codebase consume its JSON response to display recommended candidates.

src/app/api/attendance/check-in/route.ts.description: This file implements the POST endpoint /api/attendance/check‑in, wrapping the handler in a generic apiHandler for error/exception handling. It authenticates the caller by reading the “x‑user‑id” header and verifies the user’s role against an allowed attendance role list; unauthorized or ineligible requests receive 401/403 responses. The routine normalises the current date to midnight and queries Prisma to find an existing check‑in record for that user on that day, rejecting a second active check‑in with a 409 conflict. For a valid check‑in it fetches the user’s preferred work‑

src/app/api/attendance/check-out/route.t.description: This route handles POST /api/attendance/check‑out, requiring an “x‑user‑id” header to identify the requester; it loads the user’s check‑in for today (midnight‑normalized), returning 401 if unauthorized, 404 if no check‑in exists, or 409 if already checked out. The handler then records the current time as the checked‑out moment, updates the CheckIn record, and checks whether the user is leaving more than an hour early by comparing the clock to the user’s configured workHoursEnd time; if early, it marks the attendance record with status LEFT_EARLY and a note indicating minutes early. It logs the action via the audit system and responds with JSON containing the checked‑out timestamp and a leftEarly flag. The file relies on the shared prisma client for database access, the apiHandler wrapper for error handling, and the audit logger for audit trails, and is invoked by frontend components that perform checkout workflows or automated scripts that batch‑process attendance.

src/app/api/attendance/confirm-active/ro.description: The route exports a dynamic GET endpoint that verifies a user’s activity ping by checking the “x-user-id” header and a “pingId” query parameter; if either is missing it redirects to the login or dashboard page respectively. When the ping exists, belongs to the authenticated user, and is still pending, the handler updates its status to “RESPONDED” with a timestamp and calls `updateLastActive` to the user’s last‑active timestamp in Redis. After processing, it always redirects to the dashboard, appending a flag to indicate the confirmation succeeded. This endpoint is part of the attendance‑confirmation workflow—used by a front‑end button or link that sends a GET request with the pingId—ensuring that only legitimate, pending pings can be acknowledged. The code depends on the Prisma client for database access, a custom `apiHandler` wrapper for error handling and consistent response formatting, and a Redis operation that stores user activity pockets; other parts of the system (e.g., a client‑side component that initiates the ping confirmation) rely on the status change and the dashboard query param to update UI state. The handler’s pattern of header‑based auth, query‑parameter validation, and optimistic redirection aligns with the broader API structure in `src/app/api`.

src/app/api/attendance/management/route..description: Handles the GET endpoint for /api/attendance/management, providing today’s attendance snapshot to privileged users; it first reads the “x-user-role” header, allowing only “ADMIN” or “PROJECT_MANAGER” roles and otherwise returning a forbidden response. It normalizes the current day to midnight, then queries Prisma for all active non‑admin / client users, selecting their core fields plus any attendance record for today and any unresolved check‑ins (i.e., not yet checked‑out). The result set is sorted alphabetically by employee name. After fetching the data, the handler imports the Supabase storage helper to generate signed URLs for each employee’s profile picture, swallowing errors so missing avatars don’t break the payload. Finally it returns a JSON response with a single “data” key containing the enriched employee list. This route is consumed by the manager‑dashboard UI to display and filter attendance status, and sits behind

src/app/api/attendance/overtime/route.ts.description: This route implements POST /api/attendance/overtime, a protected API endpoint that completes a user’s shift or registers overtime when a check‑in record is in-progress. It reads an x‑user‑id header and the JSON body containing a checkInId and an action, validating that the check‑in belongs to the authenticated user and has not yet been checked out. For a “checkout” action it stamps checkedOutAt with the current time, logs the check‑out, and returns a success flag. For an “overtime” action it runs a Prisma transaction that increments the user’s overtimeHours for that date and resets notified8h so the 8‑hour reminder can fire again in the next hour, then logs the overtime start. Invalid or missing data yields 400, 403, or 409 errors. The handler is layered on a generic apiHandler utility that provides error handling and a forbidden() helper for unauthorized access; it uses the global Prisma client and an audit‑logging helper logAction. Front‑end components for shift completion (e.g., a “Checkout” or “Add 1 h” button) call this endpoint, and the API is consumed only

src/app/api/attendance/ping/route.ts.description: The file implements the POST /api/attendance/ping endpoint, allowing only users with ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER roles to trigger an activity‑check ping for a target employee. It reads the targetUserId from the request body, validates that no existing PENDING ping exists for that day, that the employee has an active check‑in ( yet checked out). If all checks pass it creates an AttendancePing record, then sends a notification with a confirm‑link and immediately posts a system message into a direct‑message chat between the manager and employee, creating the room necessary. The handler logs the action with audit logging, uses Prisma for all data access, and relies on shared utilities for notification, chat message creation, and API-wrapping. The endpoint is part of the attendance API layer and is invoked by the frontend attendance dashboard when a manager clicks “ping” on an employee’s status card, linking the UI click to this server‑side logic. The file indirectly supports the UI confirm‑active flow (via `/api/attendance/confirm-active`) and ensures consistent state across check‑ins, pings, and chat messaging.

src/app/api/attendance/route.ts.description: GET /api/attendance returns paginated attendance records with optional filtering by userId, from, to dates; it authenticates the request via x-user-id and x-user-role headers, aborting with 401 if missing. Managers (ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, HR) may supply a userId to view any user’s history; non‑managers always default to their own userId. The query object is validated against a zod schema, then used to build a Prisma where clause that applies date range filters and limits the result set to 30 per page, offsetting by (page‑1)*30. A Prisma $transaction simultaneously fetches the records (with user info) and counts the total matching rows, returning a JSON payload containing data and meta (total, page, limit, pages). The route is marked dynamic = "force‑dynamic" to prevent static rendering and uses a shared apiHandler wrapper for error handling. It relies on the Prisma client, the custom apiHandler, and Next.js server utilities, and is consumed by client‑side calls that expect {data, meta

src/app/api/attendance/status/route.ts.description: The file implements the server‑side handler for the `/api/attendance/status` GET endpoint, returning today’s attendance state for the authenticated user. It extracts the `x-user-id` header, and if absent responds with a 401 error; otherwise it normalizes today's date to midnight and queries Prisma for a `checkIn` record keyed by `userId` and `date`, selecting core timing fields plus the most recent `awayPing` via a `take:1` ordered query. Concurrently it pulls the user’s `attendance` status for the same day and the `workHoursEnd` timestamp from the user table. The response JSON bundles the `checkIn` and `attendance` objects (or `null` if missing), a computed `isCheckedIn` flag (true when a check‑in exists

src/app/api/auth/change-password/route.t.description: This file implements the POST /api/auth/change-password endpoint, enforcing authentication via an-user-id header and rate‑limiting the to five attempts per hour per user. It the request body against a strict zod schema an old password, new password (minimum length, mixed case, digit, special character), and a matching confirmation. After confirming the existence of a user and verifying the old password bcrypt, it hashes the new password, updates the user record, and then invalidates all other sessions by deleting every session for the user except the one tied to the current refresh token cookie (if present). The endpoint logs the change through a centralized audit function and returns a simple JSON success payload; on any failure it responds with specific error messages and HTTP status codes. This file depends on Prisma for database interactions, Redis for rate limiting, and various helper modules for auth cookie handling and token hashing, and is consumed by front‑end password‑change forms that post to this path, making it a central piece of user account security and session management.

src/app/api/auth/collab-token/route.ts.description: The `src/app/api/auth/collab-token/route.ts` file implements a force‑dynamic GET endpoint that retrieves the authenticated user's Hocuspocus access token from the `access_token` cookie, enabling the client‑side `HocuspocusProvider` to connect for real‑time collaboration. It relies on middleware that injects an `x-user-id` header; the handler aborts with a 403 via `forbidden()` if the header is missing. The endpoint uses `cookies()` to read the token; if the cookie is absent a 401 JSON error is returned, otherwise the token is returned in a JSON payload. The route is wrapped by a higher‑order `apiHandler` from `@/lib/api-handler` which normalizes errors, logs requests, and enforces CORS/security headers. This API is consumed by the front‑end collaboration UI (e.g., a whiteboard or chat) to fetch a fresh token on page load or reconnection.

src/app/api/auth/forgot-password/route.t.description: The route defines a force‑dynamic POST endpoint for the forgot‑password workflow, first throttling requests per IP through redis via checkRateLimit. It validates the incoming JSON against a zod schema, then queries Prisma for a user matching the email, selecting id, name, email, passwordHash, and Google connection flag. If the user is missing or Google‑connected without a local password, it leaks no information by returning a generic success JSON. Otherwise, it generates a six‑digit OTP, hashes it with bcrypt, and persists an otpToken record with a 10‑minute expiry. An OTP‑flow JWT signed with REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET is created and stored in a cookie via setFlowCookie, while Resend publishes the email containing the plain code and an expiration notice. The response always returns the generic success payload, ensuring the user cannot deduce account existence. This module is an internal API route that the frontend invokes from the forgot‑password UI, relies on prisma, redis, jwt, Resend, and util helpers, and is consumed by other auth endpoints that consume the otp_flow cookie to validate the code.

src/app/api/auth/google/callback/route.t.description: This Next.js API route handles the Google OAuth in the application, exposing a single GET endpoint. It parses the authorization `code` and signed `state` query parameters, rejects missing or invalid values, and verifies the CSRF‑protected `state` JWT using a server secret. After verification, it exchanges the code for access and refresh tokens at Google’s token endpoint, fetches the user profile via the Google UserInfo API, and then forwards both the code and profile data to the internal `/api/auth/google` endpoint via a signed POST, passing along any pre‑existing cookies if the flow is a “connect” to link an account rather than sign‑in. The response from that internal endpoint dictates the final redirect: a new user is sent to `/dashboard`, an account link succeeds to `/settings?highlight=google-connect&connected=true`, and any failure or timeout leads to user‑friendly error redirects such as `/login?error=google_*` or `/settings?highlight=google-connect&error=*`. All network requests use a `fetchWithTimeout` wrapper to avoid hanging calls, and the route relies on environment variables (`GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, `GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET`, `REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET`) and the generic `apiHandler` wrapper that standardizes error handling for this API layer. The route plays a critical part in the authentication flow: it’s the endpoint Google redirects to after the user consents,

src/app/api/auth/google/disconnect/route.description: The route src/app/api/auth/google/disconnect/route.ts provides the POST endpoint /​api​/​auth​/​google​/​disconnect, using Next.js API route conventions and the custom apiHandler wrapper to standardize error handling. When a request arrives it extracts the current user id from the x-user-id header, or returns a “forbidden” error if absent. With that id it updates the Prisma user record, setting isGoogleConnected to false and clearing the googleRefreshToken, effectively severing the OAuth link. It then records an audit event with logAction using the action type GOOGLE_DISCONNECTED, ensuring all disconnects are logged. Finally, the handler returns a JSON response { success: true }. This API is consumed by the front‑end when a logged‑in user clicks “Disconnect Google” in their account settings, and

src/app/api/auth/google/init/route.ts.description: The `/api/auth/google/init` route is a Next.js API endpoint that initiates a Google OAuth flow by generating an authorization URL and returning it in JSON; it is exported as a dynamic force‑dynamic handler to avoid static optimization and uses a helper `apiHandler` to encapsulate response handling and error catching. When the client sends a GET request, the handler pulls an optional `flow` query parameter (defaulting to `"login"`) and signs it into a JWT `state` token using the `REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET`, expiring in ten minutes, to be validated later in the callback. It then builds a scope string—adding `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file` only when the flow is `"connect"`—and constructs all OAuth parameters (client_id, redirect_uri pointing back to `/api/auth/google/callback`, response_type `code`, access_type `offline`, prompt `consent`, and the signed state). A URL to Google’s OAuth endpoint is formatted and sent back to the

src/app/api/auth/logout/route.ts.description: The `/api/auth/logout` POST API route logs a user out by invalidating their refresh token and revoking the associated session. It first reads an optional `x-user-id` header and the client’s IP from the request, then extracts the `refresh_token` cookie; if present, it hashes the token and looks up the session via Prisma. If a `x-user-id` header is supplied, it uses that ID as the effective user to delete session(s) and blacklist the token, otherwise it trusts the session’s stored user ID. The handler deletes matching sessions, stores the token hash in Redis blacklist for the token lifecycle period, and records an audit event with `logAction`. Whether or not a user ID was found, any found session tied to the token is purged. After cleanup, the response is a JSON payload `{ success: true }` with authentication cookies cleared via `clearAuthCookies`. This route relies on Prisma for session persistence, Redis for token blacklisting, and audit logging, and is invoked by client logout actions that trigger the API.

src/app/api/auth/logout-all/route.ts.description: This file implements the POST API endpoint `api/auth/logout‑all`, marked “force-dynamic”, used when a user logs out from all devices. It extracts the authenticated user ID from the request header, logs the IP for audit purposes, and then blacklists any existing JWT refresh token by hashing it and storing the hash in Redis with a TTL equal to the access‑token expiry. After blacklist, it deletes every session record in the PostgreSQL store linked to that user, effectively ending all active sessions. An audit entry labeled “LOGOUT_ALL” is written via `logAction`, recording the actor, target, and IP. Finally the handler returns a JSON success response and clears the `refresh_token` and any auth cookies using utilities from `auth-helpers`. The route relies on Prisma for DB operations, a custom `apiHandler` wrapper for error handling, `redis` for token blacklisting, token hashing from `tokens`, and auditing from `audit`. Other endpoints such as single‑session logout or token refresh will depend on the same cookie clearing logic, while client‑side logout buttons trigger this route to ensure all sessions are invalidated.

src/app/api/auth/otp/check/route.ts.description: The `/api/auth/otp/check` route provides a server‑side endpoint that validates an OTP session cookie, returning JSON with a `valid: true` flag if the cookie is present and the underlying JWT can be verified with `REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET`, otherwise it returns a 401 error with code `SESSION_EXPIRED`. It is marked `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` so Next.js

src/app/api/auth/refresh/route.ts.description: The `POST` handler at `/api/auth/refresh` rotates a refresh token: it first rate‑limits refresh attempts per IP, then retrieves the current token from the cookie and looks up the corresponding hashed session in Prisma, ensuring the user is active and the session hasn’t expired. It probes a Redis blacklist to detect theft; if a stolen token is reused outside a short grace period or the same token has already been reused, the handler revokes all sessions for the user, logs a `SESSION_COMPROMISED` audit record, and clears the auth cookies. If the request originates from a different device family (e.g., switching from mobile to desktop) the same revocation logic is triggered to guard against credential reuse across distinct environments. When the token is valid, the handler blacklists the old token with a two‑minute grace period, generates a brand‑new refresh token and access token, updates the session record with the new hash and next expiry, and returns the new tokens via secure cookies. This route relies on Prisma for DB access, Redis for blacklist state, token utilities for hashing and generation, auth helpers for cookie management, and the audit lib for action logging. The endpoint is consumed by client‑side auth logic when a user’s access token expires and needs silent renewal, integrating tightly with the refresh

src/app/api/auth/reset/check/route.ts.description: This route implements the `/api/auth/reset/check` endpoint used in the password‑reset workflow; it validates that a current reset session is still active by reading the `reset_flow` cookie, decoding it with the JWT `REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET`, and returning a 200 with `{valid:true}` when the token is fresh. If the cookie is missing or fails verification, it responds with a 401 and a `SESSION_EXPIRED` error code, blocking further reset actions. The handler is wrapped by a generic `apiHandler` middleware for standardized error handling and status codes. It relies on `getCookie` from the auth‑helpers library and the core `jsonwebtoken` package for token validation. The client UI calls this endpoint before rendering the reset‑password form to ensure the user remains in an active reset session, preventing replay attacks or expired token usage. Because it lives under `src/app/api/auth/reset/check`, any component or page that initiates a password reset workflow can depend on this route to gate further backend actions, and other API or client code may call it to fetch the session state.

src/app/api/auth/reset-password/route.ts.description: The `POST` handler in `src/app/api/auth/reset-password/route.ts` processes the final step of a password‑reset workflow: it validates a rate‑limited request, confirms the presence and validity of a `reset_flow` cookie (a signed JWT containing the user’s ID), and parses the new password from the request body using Zod to enforce strength rules and confirm matching fields. Upon validation, it bcrypt‑hashes the new password, generates a fresh access and refresh token pair, deletes any existing sessions for that user in the Prisma database, and creates a new session record with device and IP metadata. The handler logs the password‑reset action via `logAction`, clears the reset cookie, and sets the new auth cookies on the response. This endpoint is dynamically routed by Next.js (`dynamic = "force-dynamic"`), relies on Redis for rate‑limit checks, Prisma for persistence, token utilities for JWT handling and

src/app/api/auth/verify-otp/route.ts.description: This route handles the POST endpoint `api/auth/verify-otp` for the password‑reset OTP verification flow, enforcing IP rate limiting (10 calls/15 min) via Redis, validating a hidden `otp_flow`

src/app/api/chat/messages/[id]/route.ts.description: This file implements the server‑side handler for the DELETE HTTP method on the endpoint `/api/chat/messages/[id]`, enforcing dynamic routing. It retrieves the current user’s ID and role from request headers, fetching the target message by `id` to verify existence and ownership; only the original sender or an administrator may proceed. Upon authorization, it sets the message’s `deletedAt` timestamp and optionally clears its content via a Prisma `update`. After completing the database mutation, it emits a real‑time `message_deleted` event on the Socket.IO `/chat` namespace to notify all members of the message’s room. The handler uses a higher‑order `apiHandler` wrapper for consistent error handling and relies on Prisma for data access and the global Socket.IO instance for broadcasting. This route is critical for the chat UI’s “delete message” workflow and is called by client‑side components when a user or admin triggers deletion through the UI. It integrates tightly with the Prisma schema, the API‑handler utilities for standardized responses, and the real‑time infrastructure used elsewhere in the chat module.

src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/leave/ro.description: The `POST` export in src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/leave/route.ts provides an authenticated API endpoint that lets a user leave a chat room; it reads the user id from the `x-user-id` header via `apiHandler`, validates that the user is currently a member of the specified room using Prisma, and rejects the request if they are not or if the room type is `general_dm` (preventing direct message leave). If the membership exists, the handler deletes the `chatRoomMember` record, logs the leave action with `logAction` for auditability, and responds with a JSON success payload. The route is marked `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` so Next.js performs the request on every call rather than static rendering. It depends on the generic `api-handler` helper for error handling, the Prisma client for database access, and the audit logger; other routes such as join or message creation depend on the same Prisma models, while front‑end components (e.g., a “Leave Room” button) call this endpoint to remove a user from a room. This file therefore implements the server‑side removal workflow for chat room memberships, enforcing permissions via header‑based auth and providing immediate side‑effects (database update and audit log) that other parts of the system rely on.

src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/links/ro.description: The `GET` export in this API route serves as a protected endpoint for the chat room links dashboard, exposing all URLs posted in a to members. When a request arrives, it reads the `x-user-id` header, otherwise it rejects the request with a 403 via the shared `forbidden()` helper from `@/lib/api-handler`. It then verifies membership by querying `prisma.chatRoomMember` for a record matching the provided `roomId` and `userId`; absence of a match again returns forbidden. Next, it queries `prisma.message` for all non‑deleted messages in the room whose content contains “http”, ordering them newest first and selecting message id, content, timestamp, and sender name, while omitting any deleted messages. Each message’s content is parsed with a simple HTTP URL regex, flattening the results into an array of objects that pair the URL with the originating message’s id, sender info, and creation time. Finally, the handler responds with a JSON payload under the `data` key containing this list of link objects. This route is consumed by the client-side link‑listing component, building the UI that lets room members browse shared URLs, and it relies on the generic `apiHandler` wrapper, the Prisma ORM, and the `next/server` request/response types.

src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/media/ro.description: This file defines a forced‑dynamic Next.js API endpoint at `/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/media` that returns all non‑deleted media messages (image, video, voice, document) for a given chat room. On a GET request it extracts the authenticated user ID from the `x-user-id` header, rejecting the request with a 403 if missing, and verifies that the caller is a member of the room via Prisma’s `chatRoomMember` table. If the membership check passes, it queries the `message` table for media objects belonging to that room, filtering out null `mediaUrl` and deleted messages, ordering results newest first, and projecting minimal fields (id, mediaUrl, mediaType, createdAt, and sender name). The response is a JSON payload wrapping the list under `data`. This route is consumed by the chat UI to populate the media gallery and relies on the shared `apiHandler` wrapper for standardized error handling,

src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/messages.description: The route file implements the server‑side API for listing and creating chat messages in a specific room, exposed at `/api/chat/rooms/:roomId/messages` and forced “force-dynamic” for Next.js. In the GET handler it authenticates the user via an `x-user-id` header, verifies room membership with Prisma, paginates messages (50 per page) with optional cursor support, updates the member’s `lastReadAt`, then filters out any messages sent by `SUPER_ADMIN` and, for clients, hides messages from non‑admin roles before returning the list sorted oldest first; the response also includes a `nextCursor` for infinite scroll. The POST handler similarly authenticates the user, checks that the room exists and is not deleted, applies an admins‑only posting rule for custom groups where `adminsOnlyPosting` is set, validates the request body against a Zod schema that requires text or media, creates a new message via `createNormalChatMessage`, logs the action with `logAction` (capturing user role, IP, and whether media was attached), and finally returns the new message. Both handlers rely on `apiHandler` for

src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/recordin.description: The `route.ts` file defines the dynamic GET API handler for `/api/chat/rooms/:roomId/recordings`, exposing a list of meeting recordings to a chat room’s participants. It first retrieves the authenticated `x-user-id` header, aborting with a forbidden response if absent; then confirms the user is a member of the specified room via `prisma.chatRoomMember.findUnique`. If the membership check fails, it immediately returns forbidden. On success, it queries `prisma.meetingRecording` for all meetings belonging to that chat room, ordering by creation time and selecting metadata plus the uploading user’s name. The data returned is wrapped in `NextResponse.json({ data: recordings })`. The file relies on the shared `apiHandler` wrapper for error handling, `forbidden` helper for quick 403s, and the global `prisma` client, and is consumed by front‑end components that display a recordings list or allow actions like play or download for authorized users.

src/app/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]/route.ts.description: The route file implements the server‑side logic for deleting or updating a chat room via the dynamic API path `/api/chat/rooms/[roomId]`. Using Next.js’s `apiHandler` wrapper, it extracts the
