src/app/api/chat/rooms/custom-group/[roo.description: The `PATCH /api/chat/rooms/custom-group/[roomId]` endpoint updates a custom chat group’s metadata and membership. It first authenticates the caller through `x-user-id` and `x-user-role` headers, rejecting unknown users or those lacking ADMIN/PROJECT_MANAGER or group‑admin rights; the must exist and be of type `custom_group`. request body is validated with Zod to require a non‑empty name, a list of member IDs, optional admin IDs, and posting permissions; tool also removes any members whose role would be CLIENT or SUPER_ADMIN. Inside a Prisma transaction the handler updates the room’s name, avatar, and posting flag, deletes any members removed by the client, and upserts remaining members with their admin status, guaranteeing the requesting user stays in the group. An audit log records the change with the requester's IP and session. Finally, the handler returns `{ success: true }`. This file relies on `@/lib/api-handler` for error handling, `@/lib/prisma` for DB access, and audit utilities, and is called by the front‑end group‑editing workflow in `src/app/chat/rooms/custom-group/[roomId]/edit` to persist form changes.

src/app/api/chat/rooms/custom-group/rout.description: The `src/app/api/chat/rooms/custom-group/route.ts` file implements the backend endpoint for creating a new custom‑group chat room. It exposes a POST handler that first extracts the user id and role from custom headers and enforces that only users with role “ADMIN” or “PROJECT_MANAGER” may create a group, otherwise it throws a forbidden error. The request body is validated with a Zod schema ensuring a non‑empty name, at least one member, an optional avatar URL, and a boolean flag for admin‑only posting. The creator is automatically added to the member list and their role is set as the group admin; the handler then queries Prisma to fetch all participating users and blocks any client or super‑admin accounts from joining. A Prisma `chatRoom.create` call persists the room and a nested membership array, returning the full room object with user details. After persistence, an audit log entry is written via `logAction`—including the creator’s ID, action type, room ID, payload, and client IP extracted by `getClientIp`. The endpoint is declared as `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to always execute server‑

src/app/api/chat/rooms/dm/route.ts.description: This route handles POST requests to create or retrieve a one‑to‑one “general_dm” chat room between the authenticated user (extracted from the “x‑user‑id” header) and a target user specified in the JSON body. It first validates the payload with Zod, rejects self‑DMS, then enforces role‑based DM permissions: clients may only message admins or project managers, while only admins and managers can message clients. After the checks, it queries Prisma for an existing DM room that contains exactly the two members and returns it along with the most recent non‑deleted message; if none exists, it creates a new room linking the two users. The endpoint returns a 200 response with the room data and an 201 status on creation, along with `unreadCount: 0`. The file relies on the shared `apiHandler` wrapper and `forbidden` helper for authentication, uses Prisma for database access, and defines the route under `next/server` with `dynamic="force-dynamic"`. It is consumed by the client when initiating a DM through the chat UI and is a key part of the chat service layer in the codebase.

src/app/api/chat/rooms/route.ts.description: The `GET` handler in `src/app/api/chat/rooms/route.ts` serves the `/api/chat/rooms` endpoint, accepting an authenticated request via `x‑user‑id` and `x‑user‑role` headers. It optionally enforces the existence of project‑scoped chat rooms when a `projectId` query parameter is supplied, using `ensureProjectChatRooms`. It then builds a Prisma query that filters rooms by membership, deletion status, optional `projectId`, and an optional `scope=general` parameter that limits the results to predefined “general” room types. After retrieving the room list it calculates each room’s unread message count based on the user’s last‑read timestamp stored in `ChatRoomMember.lastReadAt`. The response is further filtered by role: clients may only see DMs with admins/PMs and never general “All Hands” groups; non‑managers cannot view DMs involving a client. Finally, for every room it removes `SUPER_ADMIN` members and, for client viewers, hides all non‑manager members before serializing the data to JSON. This route depends on the Prisma client, the generic `apiHandler` wrapper, and the project chat initialization helper, and it is consumed by the chat UI components that render the room list and unread counts on the `/chat` page.

src/app/api/chat/upload/route.ts.description: The `POST /api/chat/upload` endpoint enforces authenticated uploads by extracting `x-user-id` from headers (denied with `forbidden` if missing), then parses a multipart form containing `file` and optional `type` (defaulting to “document”) to validate the payload. It checks the file size against per‑type limits defined in `MAX_SIZES` and rejects oversized files with a 400 error. For accepted files it generates a unique storage path under `chat-media/`, writes the binary content to Supabase storage via `uploadFile`, and immediately creates a 24‑hour signed URL with `getSignedUrl`. The response returns JSON with the storage path, signed URL, and type, allowing front‑end components to embed the media in chat messages. This route depends on the generic `apiHandler` wrapper (providing error handling and response utilities) and Supabase storage helpers, while other parts of the chat feature call `/api/chat/upload` when users send attachments, enabling real‑time media display and persistence. The implementation follows Next.js 13 API route conventions, uses `NextRequest`/`NextResponse`, and relies on the custom headers for authentication rather than sessions.

src/app/api/contracts/[id]/route.ts.description: The route file implements the GET and PATCH endpoints for contract details under /api/contracts/[id].

src/app/api/contracts/[id]/sign/route.ts.description: This file implements a Next.js API route that allows an employee to sign their own contract via a POST to /api/contracts/[id]/sign. It extracts the user ID from the X‑User‑ID header and the request IP, then retrieves the contract by ID from Prisma, ensuring the contract exists, belongs to the requesting user, and is not already signed. If any check fails, it returns a specific JSON error with the appropriate HTTP status. Otherwise, it updates the contract’s status to SIGNED, logs the action using logAction (capturing user, event, contract ID, version, and IP), and returns the updated contract in JSON. The route uses the custom apiHandler wrapper for error handling and enforces dynamic rendering with dynamic = "force‑dynamic". It depends on the Prisma client, the generic apiHandler and forbidden util, and the audit logging utility, and is invoked by the contract‑detail UI when an employee clicks “Sign” on their own contract.

src/app/api/contracts/[id]/versions/rout.description: The POST handler in src/app/api/contracts/[id]/versions/route.ts implements the “upload a new contract version” endpoint. It first extracts the authenticated user’s id and role from request headers and authorizes only ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, or HR users; unauthorized requests return a 403 JSON error. It then looks up the contract by slugged id using Prisma; if missing a 404 is returned. The handler parses multipart form data for a file, validates its presence, and constructs a new storage path with the contract’s userId, incremented version number, a nanoid, and the original file extension. The file bytes are uploaded to Supabase storage via uploadFile, then a Prisma transaction simultaneously creates a new contractVersion record and updates the master contract’s currentVersion and status to PENDING, ensuring atomicity. Successful uploads return the updated contract record as JSON. This route is consumed by the contract‑management UI (e.g., a “Upload new version” form) and relies on the shared api

src/app/api/currencies/rates/route.ts.description: The `GET` handler in `src/app/api/currencies/rates/route.ts` is a force‑dynamic Next.js API route that serves the most recent currency exchange rates for an authenticated user. It extracts an `x-user-id` header; if missing, it immediately returns a 401 “AUTH_REQUIRED” error. It then queries the Prisma `currencyRate` table, ordering by `fetchedAt` descending to fetch the latest record’s `rates` JSON and timestamp. If no record exists, it falls back to a hard‑coded identity rate table with all currencies set to 1 and `fetchedAt` null. When a record is found, it parses the JSON string into a JavaScript object, guarantees a base `USD` rate of 1, and responds with

src/app/api/dashboard/route.ts.description: The `/api/dashboard` route implements a force‑dynamic GET endpoint that validates foreground auth headers (“x‑user‑id” and “x‑user‑role”), scopes the role to Prisma’s `UserRole` enum via zod, and returns a JSON payload containing dashboard‑specific data fetched by `fetchDashboardData`. The handler is wrapped by a generic `apiHandler` that normalizes errors and automatically responds with HTTP 403 if either header is missing or the role is invalid. This endpoint serves as the data source for the authenticated dashboard UI (e.g., the main page that displays user metrics and summaries), ensuring fresh data on each request rather than

src/app/api/hr/attendance-alerts/route.t.description: The GET endpoint at /api/hr/attendance‑alerts provides manager‑only alerts on employee attendance. It first verifies a x‑user‑id header, rejects other roles via apiHandler and forbidden(), and then queries Prisma for all attendance rows in the past seven days. The records are grouped by user, and for each user the dates of “ABSENT” and “VERY_LATE” statuses are collected; an alert is emitted if a user has two or more absences or three or more very‑late entries. Alerts include the user object, alert type, count, and the relevant dates, and the list is sorted by severity (count descending) before being returned in a JSON payload. The route relies on Next.js server functions, Prisma for data access, and a shared apiHandler wrapper for standardized error handling; other parts of the HR UI consume its output to display recent attendance issues to project managers and admins.

src/app/api/hr/employees/route.ts.description: This API route exposes a protected GET endpoint for HR dashboards that returns a curated list of active employees excluding admins, project managers, and clients; it first verifies the request contains an x‑user‑id header and a role in the HR_ROLES set, otherwise it returns a 401 or forbidden error via the shared apiHandler wrapper. The route queries Prisma’s user model to pull personal data, recent (last 30 days) attendance, and associated task assignments, filtering out excluded roles and only active users. For each employee it asynchronously generates a signed Supabase storage URL for their avatar if present, logs failures, then computes punctuality as the percentage of days marked PRESENT or LEFT_EARLY versus total days, and task completion metrics as the count of DONE tasks over total assigned. The final JSON payload bundles these calculations into a metrics object per employee, suited for consumption by an HR dashboard UI that displays employee cards with avatar, contact, role, and performance stats. The file depends on @/lib/prisma for DB access, @/lib/api-handler for standardized error handling, and @/lib/supabase-storage for signed URL generation; other parts of the codebase, such as HR front‑end pages, would call this route to populate the employee list and metrics. The implementation uses async Promise.all to parallelize avatar URL fetches, and defensive error logging ensures partial failures don’t break the response stream.

src/app/api/hr/interviews/[id]/start/rou.description: This file implements the POST handler for `/api/hr/interviews/[id]/start`, enforcing that only users with HR-related roles (`ADMIN`, `PROJECT_MANAGER`, `HR`) can trigger the by reading the `x-user-role` header and rejecting others with a forbidden response. It resolves the interview ID from the URL parameter, then queries Prisma for the interview record along with the candidate's basic info and the request’s stated role, and the interviewers’ names; if missing it returns the appropriate HTTP error. If the interview already has a `roomId` it simply returns that ID to avoid duplicate room creation. Otherwise it generates a UUID as a new `roomId`, updates the interview, constructs a meeting link based on `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL`, and sends a “Starting Now” email to the candidate via the Resend service, catching and logging any sending errors. The handler finally returns the new or existing `roomId` in a JSON payload. This route is part of the HR interview management back‑end, relies on Prisma for persistence, the custom `apiHandler` wrapper, and the Resend library; UI components for starting interviews trigger this endpoint and expect the returned roomId to display or join the virtual meeting.

src/app/api/hr/interviews/route.ts.description: The `route.ts` file implements a protected Next.js API route for HR interview management under `src/app/api/hr/interviews`. It exports a dynamic `GET` that, after verifying the `x-user-role` header against allowed HR roles, retrieves all interviews with status “SCHEDULED” from Prisma, including selected fields of interviewers and candidate, ordered by start time, and returns them as JSON. The `POST` endpoint validates the inbound JSON against a zod schema, ensures the start time precedes the end time, performs a conflict check across all selected interviewers (detecting overlapping scheduled interviews), fetches the candidate and their role request, then creates an interview record and updates the candidate’s status inside a Prisma transaction. After the transaction, it sends a formatted confirmation email via the Resend service, catching and logging any failures. The file relies on `apiHandler` middleware for error handling and `forbidden()` for access control, uses Prisma for all data operations, and integrates a third‑party email service. This route is the backend counterpart for any UI components that display or schedule interviews, enabling HR users to view upcoming interviews and create new ones while maintaining data integrity and notifying candidates.

src/app/api/hr/requests/[id]/candidates/.description: The file implements a dynamic Next.js API route for hiring a candidate within a specific HR request, accessible only to users with “ADMIN”, “PROJECT_MANAGER”, or “HR” roles via the `x-user-role` header; it validates the request body with Zod, ensuring salary, work mode, and member since values are present. It retrieves the candidate and request IDs from the URL parameters and confirms the candidate exists and is not already hired, preventing duplicate hires. It then checks if the candidate’s email is already registered in the system, returning a conflict error if so. Within a Prisma transaction it marks the candidate’s status as “HIRED”, creates a user record without a password, upserts a notification preference, and issues an invitation token that expires in seven days. After committing, it emails the candidate a welcome invitation via Resend, generating a secure link that allows the new user to set their password. This route sits under `/api/hr/requests/[id]/candidates/[cId]/hire`, integrates with `@/lib/prisma` for database access, `@/lib/api-handler` for common error handling, and `@/lib/api-handler`’s `forbidden` helper to enforce role-based access. The endpoint returns the created user data on success and is expected to be called from the HR dashboard or candidate management UI after a hiring action.

This file implements the PATCH handler for `/api/hr/requests/:id/candidates/:cId`, allowing HR users to update a candidate’s status, recommendation flags, internal notes, and interview date. It first authenticates a user from the `x-user-id` header, restricts access to the roles “ADMIN”, “PROJECT_MANAGER

src/app/api/hr/requests/[id]/route.ts.description: The `src/app/api/hr/requests/[id]/route.ts` exposes a server‑side endpoint for retrieving and updating individual hiring requests by ID. The GET handler authenticates the user via `x-user-id` and `x-user-role`, only allowing HR, project managers, or admins, then pulls a richly populated record from Prisma—including questions, candidate responses, interview schedules, and related user metadata—and returns it as JSON. The PATCH handler validates a comprehensive Zod schema for optional status changes, title, description, deadline, and approval flags, then enforces role constraints (only managers/admins may toggle approval flags) and status guardrails (published requests become read‑only except for status moves). It handles publishing logic by generating a unique `publicSlug` with `nanoid` when opening a request and restores to draft on unpublishing, while also ensuring that attempts to edit a published request without unpublishing result in a 400 error. After performing the update, it logs the action through `logAction` for audit purposes and

src/app/api/hr/stats/route.ts.description: The /api/hr/stats route exposes a server‑side GET endpoint that forces dynamic rendering and requires an “x‑user‑role” header; if the role is not ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, or HR the request is rejected via a forbidden error. It calculates three statistical reports from the Prisma database: a hiring funnel that aggregates candidate counts by status into applied, shortlisted, interviewed, hired, and rejected buckets; an

src/app/api/invite/[token]/route.ts.description: This file exposes the GET endpoint `/api/invite/[token]`, wrapping the handler in a generic `apiHandler` that standardizes error handling and typing. It first extracts the invitation token from route params, validates its presence, and queries the Prisma `invitation` table for a matching record. The handler then enforces several business rules: it rejects nonexistent invites, already‑accepted invites, and those past the `expiresAt` timestamp, returning appropriate HTTP status codes and error payloads. If the invite passes validation, the invitor's `name` and `email` are fetched from the `user` table, allowing the API to return a minimal invitation payload (`email`, `role`, `inviterName`, `inviterEmail`, `expiresAt`) for the front‑end to display or process. This route supports the invitation confirmation workflow, typically invoked when a new user clicks a link in an invitation email. It depends on the shared `prisma` client and the higher‑level `apiHandler`; other parts of the codebase, such as the invite acceptance UI, consume its JSON output. The use of `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` ensures that Next.js

src/app/api/invite/accept/route.ts.description: The file implements the POST /api/invite/accept endpoint, validating the request body against a Zod schema that requires a token, a name matching a simple alphabetic regex, and a minimum‑length password. It then queries the Prisma database for an invitation matching the token, ensuring it hasn’t been used, is not expired, and that the email is not already registered; failing any check returns a 4xx JSON error. Once validated, it hashes the password with bcrypt, captures the request IP and user‑agent, and runs a transaction that creates a new user record with the invitation’s role, creates a default notification preference, and marks the invitation as accepted. The handler generates a device ID, a hashed refresh token, and an access token, creates a session row with device and request metadata, and sets both tokens in secure cookies via setAuthCookies. It logs the action using logAction, then returns a JSON payload containing the user’s id, name, and role. This file depends on the shared prisma client, token utilities, auth helper, audit logger, and the API handler wrapper, and it is called by frontend flows that let new users accept emailed invites,

src/app/api/meetings/[id]/participants/r.description: This file implements the GET endpoint at api/meetings/[id]/participants using Next.js middleware. It first extracts the authenticated user’s ID and role from request headers, then validates access to the meeting via `assertMeetingAccess`, which queries Prisma for the meeting and depending on its type (project, workspace, task, or interview) checks that the user is an authorized member, assignee, creator, or interviewer, or that the user is a manager; otherwise it returns 403 or 404/410 errors. If access is granted, it fetches all active `meetingParticipant` rows (where leftAt is null), includes user details, and returns them as a concise list of `{id,name,role,profilePicUrl}` objects in JSON. The `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` flag enforces non‑caching per request. This route underpins front‑end participant listings or call‑in screens and relies on shared utilities (`apiHandler

src/app/api/meetings/[id]/presence/route.description: The file implements a dynamic Next.js API route at `/api/meetings/[id]/presence` that manages meeting presence for the authenticated user. `GET` returns whether the current user is inside the meeting, along with timestamps of when they joined or left, after validating access via `assertMeetingAccess`. `POST` records a user’s join event by upserting a `meetingParticipant` record, timestamps the join time, and writes a “MEETING_JOINED” audit entry. `DELETE` records the user’s leave by setting `leftAt`, logs a “MEETING_LEFT” event, and if no participants remain it automatically ends the meeting, logging “MEETING_AUTO_ENDED”. Access control checks the user’s role against a list of manager roles or membership in the associated project, workspace, task, or interview, returning a 403 for forbidden cases. The route relies on `prisma` for database access, `apiHandler` for error wrapping, and the `logAction` audit helper, and is consumed by front‑end components that track real‑time attendance or display a presence status indicator for meeting participants.

src/app/api/meetings/[id]/recording/rout.description: This endpoint implements the POST /api/meetings/:id/recording API used when a user attaches a recording to an existing meeting. It first verifies the caller’s identity via an `x-user-id` header, rejecting requests with a 401 status if missing. The meeting identifier comes from the route parameters and is validated; a missing ID yields a 404 response. The request body must contain a `blobPath` (storage path for the file) and optionally a `title`; absence of `blobPath` returns a 400 error. The handler then persists a new `meetingRecording` record with the meeting ID, uploader ID, storage path, and title (defaulting to “Meeting Recording” if omitted). After writing to the database, it logs the create action via `logAction`, recording the user, action type, entity, and related meeting ID. Finally, it responds with `{ success: true }`. The route relies on the shared `prisma` client and audit logging, and is called by front‑end components that upload the recording either directly to storage or through a separate chat upload flow, integrating tightly with the meeting detail UI where users

src/app/api/meetings/[id]/route.ts.description: The `route.ts` file implements the PATCH and DELETE HTTP handlers for the `/api/meetings/[id]` endpoint, enforcing that only users with "ADMIN" or "PROJECT_MANAGER" roles may modify or remove a meeting. It extracts the user ID and role from request headers, validates them, and aborts with a 401 if the user is unauthenticated or a 403 if the role is unsuitable, with a helper `forbidden()` from `api-handler`. Upon receiving a PATCH request, the handler validates the payload against a `zod` schema that allows an optional title and optional scheduledAt (which can be null), then updates the meeting record via Prisma, returning the updated fields. If the meeting

src/app/api/meetings/recordings/route.ts.description: The `/api/meetings/recordings` collection route exposes a dynamic GET endpoint that lists meeting recording entries for an authenticated user. It first verifies a custom `x-user-id` header, returning a 401 if absent, then parses optional `meetingId`, `projectId`, `workspaceId`, and `taskId` query parameters to build a Prisma `OR` filter. Unspecified parameters are omitted via a `filter` step that removes undefined values, guaranteeing that only active criteria are applied. The handler queries `prisma.meetingRecording`, eager‑loading the uploader’s name and the related meeting title, sorting results by `createdAt` descending. Results are wrapped in a JSON object under `data` and returned with `NextResponse.json`. This route is used by UI pages that display recordings, feeding lists where users can filter by meeting, project, workspace, or task context. The file relies on the shared Prisma client (`@/lib/prisma`) and a robust `api

src/app/api/meetings/recordings/upload/r.description: The file exposes a POST handler for the /api/meetings/recordings/upload route in a Next.js API route. It validates the incoming JSON payload against a Zod schema that includes a meetingId, optional title, storagePath, optional sizeBytes, optional durationSec, and uploadedById. Upon successful validation it performs a single Prisma create operation to persist a new MeetingRecording record, converting sizeBytes to a BigInt if supplied, and linking the record to its meeting and uploader. Immediately after creation it calls a shared audit utility to log the “MEETING_RECORDING_UPLOADED” event, passing the uploader ID, the new record’s ID, and the meeting association. Finally it returns the newly created record wrapped in a NextResponse JSON payload. The route relies on the global prisma client, a generic `apiHandler` wrapper that likely handles errors and filterions, and an audit logger; it is intended to be called by a trusted internal service such as Jibri, though the current code does not enforce authentication. The endpoint is a backend side‑effect point used by external recording ingest services and can be tested independently in unit or integration tests that mock Prisma and the audit logger.

src/app/api/meetings/route.ts.description: The route.ts file implements the GET handler for the `/api/meetings` endpoint, used to fetch a project’s meetings. It first extracts `x-user-id` and `x-user-role` headers for authentication; absent `x-user-id` returns a 401. The handler expects a required `projectId` query parameter, otherwise returning 400. Role logic distinguishes managers (ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, SUPER_ADMIN) who see all meetings, clients who see only client meetings, and non‑manager team members who see non‑client meetings, client meetings they were invited to, or client meetings that have already ended, encaps

src/app/api/meetings/start/route.ts.description: The `/api/meetings/start` POST route authenticates the user through the `x‑user‑id` header, validates the request body via Zod, and enforces role‑based access: clients cannot start meetings, and only admin or project‑manager roles can schedule client‑centric meetings; it checks membership against a project, workspace, or task to ensure the initiator has access. Once authorized, it determines the appropriate chat room (provided, the default project team room, or workspace room), creates a unique LiveKit room ID, inserts a meeting record into Prisma, and for client meetings it bulk‑creates

src/app/api/notifications/mark-all-read/.description: The `POST /api/notifications/mark-all-read` endpoint reads the authenticated user’s ID from the `x-user-id` request header, failing with a 403 via `forbidden()` if the header is absent. It then marks every unread notification for that user as read by performing a bulk `prisma.notification.updateMany` with `where: { userId, isRead: false }`. After updating the database, the handler checks for a globally initialized Socket.IO instance (`global.io`), and if present it emits a `has_unread` event with `false` to the room specific to the user (`user:${userId}`) inside the `/notifications` namespace. Finally, it responds with `{ success: true }` in JSON using `NextResponse`. This route is invoked by the client‑side notifications UI when a user chooses to clear all alerts, ensuring both persistent state and real‑time UI update. It relies on the helper wrappers `apiHandler` for error

src/app/api/notifications/route.ts.description: This file implements the paginated notifications API endpoint for GET /notifications, forcing dynamic rendering so the server always pulls fresh data. It extracts the authenticated user’s ID from the x‑user‑id header, rejecting the request if missing. Query parameters `page` and `limit` are clamped to sane ranges, then a single Prisma query fetches that slice of the user’s notifications sorted newest first while another counts the total for pagination metadata. After retrieving the data, notifications are grouped into human‑readable day labels (“Today”, “Yesterday”, or a formatted date) using dayjs for the frontend to display them hierarchically. The handler returns JSON with `notifications`, `groups`, `total`, `page`, and `limit` so the client can render a grouped list and navigate pages. It relies on the shared `apiHandler` wrapper for consistent error handling,

src/app/api/presence/route.ts.description: The `/api/presence` endpoint exposes a dynamic GET route that retrieves the current online user set from Redis; it forces the route to be cache‑bypassed by setting `dynamic = "force-dynamic"`. The exported `GET` handler is wrapped with a custom `apiHandler

src/app/api/projects/[id]/documents/[doc.description: This route file implements the CRUD API for a single document inside a project, exposing GET, PATCH, and DELETE handlers that are wrapped by a custom `apiHandler` middleware for standard error handling and rate limiting. The GET endpoint retrieves a document by its ID, joins milestone and creator data, enforces access restrictions based on document visibility (PRIVATE, INTERNAL, CLIENT_VIEW/EDIT) and the caller’s role or owner status, and returns 401 if the caller is unauthenticated or 403 if unauthorized. The PATCH endpoint validates title and access fields with Zod, allows managers to change any field, owners to rename their document, logs a `DOCUMENT_UPDATED` audit event, and returns the updated record. The DELETE endpoint is restricted to manager roles, prevents deletion of system “requirements” documents, logs a `DOCUMENT_DELETED` action, and responds with a success flag. The file relies on `next/server` for request/response objects, a Prisma client for database access, and a custom audit logger, fitting into the backend layer that other API routes (e.g., project or milestone routes) depend on for security and data consistency. This implementation follows a role‑based access control pattern and uses the forced dynamic export to ensure every invocation goes through the latest code.

This file implements the `/api/projects/[id]/documents/[docId]/share` POST handler in a Next.js app, wrapping its logic in a custom `apiHandler` middleware. It retrieves the `projectId` and `docId` from the context’s route parameters and rejects the request with a 404 if either is missing. The handler parses a JSON body containing an `action` that can be `"enable"`, `"disable"`, or `"regenerate"`, queries Prisma for the document with the given `id` and `projectId`, and returns a 404 if not found. Depending on the action, it sets `isShared` to true (also ensuring a 12‑character `shareToken` generated with `nanoid` if absent), sets `isShared` to false, or replaces the token with a new `nanoid` value. It updates the document in the database with these changes, then returns the updated record in JSON. The endpoint is consumed by frontend controls that toggle a document’s share status or reset its link, and it relies on the project's Prisma schema and the `apiHandler` for error handling.

src/app/api/projects/[id]/documents/rout.description: This file implements the REST endpoints for listing and creating documents inside a project identified by `[id]` in the Next.js app router. The GET handler authenticates the user via the `x‑user‑id` header, determines their role (`ADMIN`, `PROJECT_MANAGER`, `CLIENT`, or a project member), and then fetches documents from Prisma with access rules that differ by role: managers retrieve all docs, clients see only `CLIENT_VIEW`/`CLIENT_EDIT` docs, and team members see `INTERNAL`, `CLIENT_VIEW`, `CLIENT_EDIT`, and their own `PRIVATE` docs; the response includes metadata such as milestone, share token, and initial content. The POST handler blocks clients, parses the request body with Zod, creates a new document (assigning `ownerId` only for private docs), logs the creation via `logAction`, and returns the new document. Auth decisions are made purely from request headers, while output shape is hard‑coded through Prisma selects. The module depends on `@/lib/prisma`

src/app/api/projects/[id]/milestones/[mI.description: The src/app/api/projects/[id]/milestones/[mId]/route.ts file implements a protected PATCH endpoint that updates a milestone’s title, content, or status; it is invoked by the project‑milestone UI when a manager edits a milestone. After extracting the user ID and role from request headers, it ensures the caller is an ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER, otherwise returning 401 or a 403 via `forbidden()`. It validates the request body with Zod, confirming optional fields conform to the defined schema, and then fetches the milestone by ID to confirm it belongs to the specified project. The handler builds a minimal `updateData` payload, sets `completedAt` if the status becomes COMPLETED, and applies it via Prisma. When a milestone is marked finished, it checks for any remaining non‑completed milestones; if none exist, it automatically marks the parent project as COMPLETED. Throughout, it logs the change with `logAction`, ensuring audit

src/app/api/projects/[id]/questions/[qId.description: This route handles a POST request to `/api/projects/[id]/questions/[qId]/answer`, allowing a client or project manager to submit an answer to a question. It extracts the user ID and role from request headers and the projectId and qId from the URL, returning a 400 error if missing and a 403 if the user is neither the project’s client nor a manager. Using Zod, it validates the answer content (1‑5000 characters). A new `QuestionAnswer` record is created in Prisma, including the user reference, and an audit log is written via `logAction`. Based on the actor, notifications are dispatched: a client’s reply alerts all project managers and an admin; a manager’s reply notifies the client. Finally, it returns the created answer as JSON with a 201 status. This endpoint relies on the shared `apiHandler` wrapper, Prisma for data access, and the notification and audit utilities, and is consumed by the project

This handler implements the PATCH endpoint at /​api​/projects/​{id}​/questions/​{qId}​/approve for marking a project as approved. It extracts the authenticated user ID from the x‑user‑id header and enforces that only users with the ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER role may perform the action, otherwise returning a 403. The project and question identifiers come from the route params; the handler validates their presence and uses Prisma to set isApproved to true via updateMany, returning a 404 if no rows are updated. Upon success it logs an audit record, then emits a question_approved event over Socket.IO on the /project_questions:{projectId} room so all connected clients instantly receive the change. Dependencies include next/server, a custom apiHandler wrapper that handles request parsing and error formatting, prisma for database interaction, and logAction for audit logging; any frontend component that displays questions can trigger this route and listen for the socket event to update its UI. The global io socket is used only when available, ensuring server‑side broadcast even if the client’s relay fails.

src/app/api/projects/[id]/questions/rout.description: This file implements the `/api/projects/[id]/questions` endpoint handling GET and POST requests for project‑level questions. The GET handler authenticates the user via `x‑user‑id` and `x‑user‑role`, verifies membership, the client, or a manager role, and returns a list of questions (filtered to approved ones for clients) with nested answers, milestone, and creator data, ordered by date. The POST handler requires the requester not be a CLIENT, validates the question payload with Zod, and inserts a new question into the database, auto‑approving it for managers but marking it pending otherwise; it then logs the action and notifies all project managers if the submitter isn’t a manager. The module relies on custom `apiHandler` and `forbidden` helpers for uniform error handling, Prisma for data access, a notification service, and an audit logger. It is consumed by front‑end components that display question lists and allow creation, and it integrates tightly with project‑membership, role‑based access control, and notification flows in the broader codebase

src/app/api/projects/[id]/route.ts.description: This file defines the dynamic API route /‑project/[id] for reading and updating a project. The GET handler authenticates the user via x‑user‑id/x‑user‑role headers, validates access with canAccess, and fetches the project, its milestones, member list (excluding SUPER_ADMINs) with signed avatar URLs, client details, AI context content, unread messages count, and number of unanswered questions; it composes these into a JSON response. The PATCH handler restricts modifications to ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER roles, parses a zod schema, and inside a Prisma transaction updates the project fields, synchronizes or creates the AI context, replaces the team member list while preserving admin/manager assignments, removes departed members from active tasks, handles milestone CRUD (updating or creating titles, content, order, status, completion timestamps, and related “milestone_doc” documents), and deletes unused milestone docs/records; it then ensures chat rooms exist for the project. It

src/app/api/projects/[id]/tasks/route.ts.description: This file exposes the server‑side API for the `/api/projects/[id]/tasks` endpoint, wrapping both GET and POST handlers with a common `apiHandler` to normalize error handling. The GET handler authenticates the request using the `x-user-id` and `x-user-role` headers, preventing `CLIENT` roles, and authorizes via a check against project membership or manager status, then returns the task list sorted by newest, including assignee users and created‑by data. The POST handler similarly authenticates, validates the request body against a Zod schema that requires a non‑empty title, then automatically assigns all active `ADMIN` and `PROJECT_MANAGER` members of the project plus the creator by creating an assignee link via a Prisma transaction before creating the task record. After creation it triggers an audit log via `logAction`. The file depends on `@/lib/api-handler`, `@/lib/prisma`, and `@/lib/audit`; higher‑level UI components (e.g., a project tasks page) call these endpoints to display tasks or to create a new one. The dynamic export forces Next.js to re‑evaluate the route on each request, ensuring fresh authorization checks. The particular patterns—header‑based auth, role checks, Prisma transactions, and audit logging

src/app/api/projects/[id]/vault/[secretI.description: The `DELETE` endpoint in `src/app/api/projects/[id]/vault/[secretId]/route.ts` implements the authorized removal of a vault secret belonging to a specific project. It is a force‑dynamic Next.js API route wrapped in `apiHandler`, which standardizes error handling and adds a CSRF‑prevention wrapper. The handler extracts `x‑user‑id` and `x‑user‑role` from request headers to authorize deletion via the `canDelete` function—only users with roles ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER, or clients who own the project, may proceed. After validating the `projectId` and `secretId` from the URL params, it checks that the secret exists and belongs to the target project, then deletes the record with Prisma, and records an audit event (`VAULT_SECRET_DELETED`) including the secret’s key payload. If the user lacks permission the helper `forbidden()` sends a 403 response, and missing parameters or non‑existent secrets return 400 or 404 errors respectively. This route is consumed by the project vault UI to allow users to delete secrets; it depends on the shared `prisma` client, audit logging utilities, and the generic `apiHandler` wrapper, and in turn is called by the front‑end when a user invokes the delete action on a vault secret.

src/app/api/projects/[id]/vault/route.ts.description: The “/api/projects/[id]/vault” route implements the server‑side vault API for a project’s secrets; it exports a forced‑dynamic handler that only runs on the server and relies on the custom `apiHandler` wrapper, Prisma for database access, and a `logAction` audit function. A user must provide `x-user-id` and `x-user-role` headers; the handlers then perform permission checks with `canRead` (ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, project client, or a project member) and `canWrite` (ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, or the client of the project). On a successful GET, the handler queries `projectSecret` records for the given project ID, orders them by creation date, and returns the data. A POST receives a JSON validated by a Zod schema, then performs a `upsert` on the `projectSecret` table, updating an existing secret or inserting a new one, and records the operation in the audit log. The route is consumed by the UI’s Secret Vault page and other components that need read/write access to project secrets; it is not a UI file itself but powers the Vault table/list and secret update flows. Overall, it demonstrates the project‑level access model, uses strict headers for auth, and integrates Prisma CRUD with an audit trail.

src/app/api/projects/client-requests/[id.description: The patch route at /app/api/projects/client-requests/[id]/ignore/force-dynamic updates a client‑project request’s status to IGNORED. It requires a POST header x‑user‑role set to ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER, failing which the apiHandler helper throws a forbidden error. The handler extracts the request id from the URL parameters (ctx?.params.id), validates it, then uses Prisma to persist `{ status: "IGNORED" }` on the corresponding database record. It responds with JSON `{ success: true }` or a 400 error for a missing id. The route is wrapped by `apiHandler` to provide consistent error handling across the API layer. This file is part of the backend service that the Projects UI calls when an administrator or manager chooses to dismiss a pending request; code elsewhere may list or filter requests by status to hide ignored ones. It depends on Next.js’s server‑only runtime, the shared prisma client, and the custom api‑handler utilities.

This file implements the GET endpoint for `/api/projects/client‑requests/[id]`, allowing privileged users (ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER) to retrieve a specific client‑project request. It leverages the custom `apiHandler` wrapper from `@/lib/api-handler` to provide standardized error handling and response formatting. The handler extracts the request ID from the route params, validates its presence, and then queries Prisma for the corresponding `clientProjectRequest`. If the request does not exist, it returns a 404; if the ID is missing, a 400. For authenticated users, it fetches the related client user record, selecting only id, name, and profilePicUrl, and combines that data with the request details before returning a 200 response. The endpoint depends on the Prisma client (`@/lib/prisma`) and the auth header `x-user-role`; other parts of the backend call this route to populate request detail pages or admin dashboards.

src/app/api/projects/client-requests/rou.description: This file implements the protected GET endpoint `/api/projects/client-requests`, exposing pending client‑project requests to the application. It first extracts the `x-user-role` header and immediately rejects anyone who is not an `ADMIN` or `PROJECT_MANAGER` by invoking `forbidden()`. Using Prisma, it queries the `clientProjectRequest` table for records with `status` = `PENDING`, ordering them by newest creation date. To avoid an N+1 problem it then batch‑loads the unique `clientId` participants via a single `findMany` on the `user` model, selecting only essential fields (`id`, `name`, `profilePicUrl`). Each request is enriched with a `client` object derived from this lookup before being wrapped in a JSON response. The route relies on the generic `apiHandler` wrapper for consistent error handling and on the centralized Prisma client exposed through `@/lib/prisma`; other parts of the system (dashboard widgets or admin panels) consume this endpoint to display or manage pending client‑project approvals.

src/app/api/projects/requests/route.ts.description: The `route.ts` file implements the POST endpoint `/api/projects/requests` for a Next.js API route that permits only authenticated users with the “CLIENT” role to submit a new project request. It parses multipart form data, validates the title and description with Zod, and optionally uploads a PDF file to Supabase Storage, rejecting non‑PDFs and files larger than 5 MB. After validation, it creates a `clientProjectRequest` record in Prisma, logs the action with `logAction`, and triggers a notification to all active users with the “ADMIN” or “PROJECT_MANAGER” roles via `sendNotification`. The response is a 201 JSON payload containing the created request object, while validation errors produce a 400 JSON response with a `VALIDATION_ERROR` code. This endpoint depends on util modules for request handling, Prisma client, Supabase storage, notification, and audit logging; other parts of the application call it from client‑side form submissions in the projects request UI to submit new projects.

src/app/api/projects/route.ts.description: The file implements the /api/projects Next.js API route, exposing GET for listing projects and POST for creating a new one; it forces dynamic runtime and authenticates users via “x‑user‑id” and “x‑user‑role” headers, blocking access if missing. GET returns all projects to ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER, all client projects to CLIENT, and only projects the user is a member of for other roles, including related milestones (ordered), members with user details, and client info through a constant Prisma include. POST validates its payload with Zod, allows only ADMIN/PROJECT_MANAGER to create, and runs a single Prisma transaction that creates the project, its AI context entry,

src/app/api/public/careers/[slug]/apply/.description: Handles POSTs to /api/public/careers/[slug]/apply by validating a public hiring request via Prisma, rejecting if the job slug is not found or closed; parses multipart form data, verifies an hCaptcha token against the secret stored in the environment, and returns explicit error codes for missing or invalid captcha. Extracts required applicant fields (name, email, phone), checks for duplicate applications, and optionally uploads a CV file, enforcing a 10 MB limit, PDF MIME and filename checks, and a "%PDF-" magic‑byte sniff before saving the file via the Supabase storage helper. Iterates through the job’s public questions, handling FILE uploads similarly to the CV and storing other field types (NUMBER, DATE, BOOLEAN, EMAIL, URL) with strict validation, requiring answers for mandatory fields and rejecting invalid data. After validation, creates a candidate record with the uploaded file URLs and answers, and triggers a non‑critical real‑time notification to all active HR‑role users through a statically mounted Socket.IO instance (global io). The route relies on the Prisma client for data access, the Supabase uploadFile abstraction, and the hCaptcha verification endpoint; other backend services (e.g., the HR dashboard) consume the events it emits. It is invoked by front‑end apply forms that submit multipart/form-data via fetch, and the API is intended to be used anonymously by anyone who can view the public career page.

src/app/api/public/careers/[slug]/route..description: The file defines a server‑side API endpoint for the dynamic route /​public/​careers/[slug] in a Next.js app, forcing the route to be re‑evaluated on every request by exporting dynamic as "force-dynamic". The exported GET handler receives a slug parameter, queries a Prisma client for a hiring request whose publicSlug matches that slug and whose status is "OPEN", projecting only the ID, role, title, description, deadline and an ordered list of questions with their IDs, texts, required flags and order. If the request is not found the handler returns a 404 JSON response with an error code; otherwise it returns a 200 JSON payload containing the selected data. This endpoint is consumed by the public careers detail page to fetch posting information before rendering the form, and it relies on the global prisma instance from @/lib/prisma. Backend logic is read‑only, includes no state mutations, and the conditional logic only handles the not‑found case. The route is tied to the UI for viewing open career postings and is the sole source of recruitment data for client‑side rendering in that section.

src/app/api/public/docs/[token]/route.ts.description: The file implements the public GET endpoint `/api/public/docs/[token]`, exposing a shared document by its unique share token. It extracts the token from the URL parameters, rejects missing tokens with a 404, and then queries Prisma for a `document

src/app/api/push/subscribe/route.ts.description: This API route, exposed at `/api/push/subscribe`, accepts a POST request containing a Web Push subscription JSON. It first pulls an `x-user-id` header to identify the authenticated user, returning a “forbidden” response via the central `apiHandler` if absent. The request body is validated against a Zod schema that requires an endpoint URL and the cryptographic `p256dh` and `auth` keys. Upon successful validation, the handler updates the corresponding record in the Prisma `User` table, storing the subscription object as a JSON string in the `pushSubscription` field. A JSON success response is then returned. This endpoint is intended to be called by front‑end code that registers a ServiceWorker subscription, enabling push notifications for that user. It relies on the shared `apiHandler` error handling and `forbidden` helper, and on the global `prisma` client, and is consumed by any component that needs to persist a user’s push subscription, such as a notification settings

src/app/api/storage/signed-url/route.ts.description: The `/src/app/api/storage/signed-url/route.ts` file exposes a server‑side GET endpoint that generates a temporary signed URL for accessing a Supabase storage bucket. It authenticates requests by the `x-user-id` header — if absent it triggers a `forbidden()` error from `@/lib/api-handler`. The handler parses the incoming request's URL to extract a required `path` query parameter, returning a 400 response if missing. It calls `getSignedUrl` (supabase-storage lib) with a 1‑hour expiry to obtain a presigned URL; a console error is logged if generation fails. Finally it returns a JSON payload `{ url: signedUrl }`. The endpoint is used by client‑side code (e.g., upload/download components) that need secure, time‑limited access to user files, tying the API to Supabase's bucket API. It relies on the shared authentication helper (`apiHandler`, `forbidden`) and the Supabase storage wrapper, and is invoked by client code via a fetch to `/api/storage/signed-url?path=...`.

src/app/api/super-admin/audit-logs/route.description: This file implements the `/api/super-admin/audit-logs` endpoint used by the Super Admin audit‑log UI; it is marked `force-dynamic` to bypass static rendering and always resolve at request time. The `superAdminOnly` helper checks the custom `x-user-role` header, returning a 404 if the caller isn’t a SUPER_ADMIN, thereby protecting the route from public access. The GET handler parses optional `action` and `entity` query parameters, then queries the Prisma `auditLog` table applying case‑insensitive `contains` filters on those fields if present. It limits results to the latest 200, orders by `createdAt` descending, and selects only the columns required by the frontend. The returned JSON (`{data: logs}`) feeds the table component in the audit‑log page, enabling server‑side pagination and filtering without exposing raw DB structure. This route depends on the global Prisma client (`@/lib/prisma`) and is called by the audit

src/app/api/super-admin/blacklist/route..description: The `/api/super-admin/blacklist` endpoint serves the admin UI’s blacklist analytics panel; it is a forced dynamic Next.js route that first verifies an `x-user-role` header equals `"SUPER_ADMIN"` and returns a 404 if not. Once authorized, it initiates a Prisma transaction that concurrently counts all refresh token blacklist rows, counts those that have expired relative to the current time, and retrieves the most recent 20 theft attempts—entries where the token was reused once—sorted by `blacklistedAt`. The response payload includes `totalCount`, `activeCount` (derived by subtracting expired entries), `expiredCount`, and the `theftAttempts` array with selected fields (`id`, `userId`, `blacklistedAt`, `graceUntil`, `expiresAt`). This route feeds the super‑admin dashboard’s statistics card and a table of recent theft incidents. It depends on the shared Prisma client (`@/lib/prisma`) and is consumed by‐client code that expects a JSON structure rather than a full page render. The implementation pattern relies on header‑based impersonation checks

src/app/api/super-admin/db-stats/route.t.description: The `route.ts` file implements the `/api/super-admin/db-stats` GET endpoint, forcing dynamic rendering and protecting it with a guard that rejects any request whose `x-user-role` header is not “SUPER_ADMIN” by returning a 404 response; the guard is isolated in `superAdminOnly` for reuse. When authorized, the handler queries a curated list of core tables—such as User, Session, Project, Task, Message, Document, Notification, and various admin‑related tables—by issuing a raw `SELECT COUNT(*)` statement to Prisma for each table concurrently via `Promise.all`. The raw query function uses `prisma.$queryRawUnsafe` and the helper type `CountRow` to parse the bigint result, which is then coerced to a JavaScript number before being packaged into a `{ table, count }` object. The response payload is a JSON object under the `data` key containing an array of these count objects. This endpoint is designed for internal monitoring dashboards or tooling that displays database population metrics to super‑admin users, and it

src/app/api/super-admin/push-test/route..description: The `src/app/api/super-admin/push-test/route.ts` file implements a protected POST endpoint that lets an authenticated SUPER_ADMIN user trigger a test push notification to any other user in the system. It first guards the request by checking an `x-user-role` header, returning a 404 if the role is not `SUPER_ADMIN`; this mirrors the role‑based checks used across the super‑admin API tier. The request body is validated with Zod to enforce a `userId`, a title (1‑100 chars), and a body (1‑500 chars), ensuring consistent payload shape before any side‑effects. Upon successful validation, it calls `sendPushNotification` from `@/lib/push` to deliver

src/app/api/super-admin/sessions/route.t.description: The `route.ts` file implements the `/api/super-admin/sessions` endpoint used exclusively by the Super Admin role; it first checks the request header `x-user-role` and returns a 404 response if the caller is not a super admin. The GET handler pulls all active sessions from the Prisma `session` table where `expiresAt` is in the future, orders them by recent usage, and includes essential meta‑data plus the related user’s id, name, email and role, then returns the result as JSON. The DELETE handler accepts a payload that is either `{sessionId}` or `{userId}` (validated with Zod) and removes either a single session or all sessions belonging to a user, logging each action through `logAction` for audit purposes and responding with the count of sessions removed.

src/app/api/super-admin/system-config/ro.description: This file implements a protected API endpoint at `/api/super-admin/system-config` that allows super‑administrators to read and modify application‑wide settings. It exports `dynamic="force-dynamic"` so every request is handled server‑side without static caching. The helper `superAdminOnly` checks the `x-user-role` header, returning a 404 if the role isn’t “SUPER_ADMIN”, thereby securing the route from other users. The GET handler retrieves all `systemConfig` rows sorted by key and returns them as JSON; the PATCH handler expects a JSON body validated by a Zod schema (`{ key, value }`), updates the corresponding record in Prisma, and records the change via `logAction` for audit purposes, using the `

src/app/api/tasks/[id]/chat-room/route.t.description: This route provides a secure, “force‑dynamic” API endpoint for the Next.js app that allows a logged‑in user to obtain or create a chat room tied to a specific task. It reads the task id from the URL param, verifies that the requester (identified by “x‑user‑id” and “x‑user‑role” headers) is authorized – managers, the task creator, or an assignee – and blocks clients. Once authenticated, it looks for an existing task‑group chat room; if none exists, it creates one, populating it with the creator and all assignees and committing the data to the Prisma database. If the room already exists, the handler ensures the current user is listed as a member by upserting a chatRoomMember record. Finally, it returns a JSON payload containing

src/app/api/tasks/[id]/route.ts.description: This file provides the API endpoint for a single task identified by `[id]` in a Next.js app, exposing GET and PATCH handlers that enforce role‑based access control via `x‑user‑id` and `x‑user‑role` headers; it blocks `CLIENT` and non‑authorized users, allowing only `ADMIN`, `PROJECT_MANAGER`, the task creator, or an assigned user to read or modify the task. The GET handler queries Prisma for the task and related data (project, assignees, chat room) and returns a 404 if not found, with 403 for unauthorized access, ensuring only managers or involved users can retrieve the full details. The PATCH handler validates the incoming payload with Zod (`title`, `description`, `status`, `projectId`, `assigneeIds`), safeguards completed tasks from assignee changes, preserves any admin or manager assignees against removal, updates status and completedAt accordingly, synchronizes chat room membership to match the new assignee list, and finally logs the update action via an audit helper. Together, the two endpoints support the task detail and edit workflows in the

src/app/api/tasks/route.ts.description: The `/api/tasks` Next.js route authorizes users via an `x-user-id` header and blocks the “CLIENT” role; it then exposes two endpoints wrapped by a generic `apiHandler`. The GET handler reads URL search parameters (`projectId`, `workspaceId`, `status`, `mine`) and builds a Prisma filter that, for non‑managers or when `mine=true`, restricts results to tasks created by the user or assigned to them, while managers can see all matching tasks; it returns selected task data with project and assignee details. The POST handler validates the request body with Zod, then uses a `$transaction` to create a new task, attach assignees, and create a related chat room, all for the authenticated user; after the transaction it sends notifications to assignees (except the creator) and writes an audit log entry. The route depends

src/app/api/users/[id]/work-hours-warnin.description: The file implements a dynamic server‑side API route at /api/users/[id]/work‑hours‑warning that returns a warning if the requested user’s current time falls outside their configured work hours. It unwraps the NextRequest via a custom apiHandler wrapper to centralize error handling and validates authentication by checking an x‑user‑id header, returning a 401 if absent. The handler extracts the target user ID from the URL params, queries Prisma for the user’s name and work‑hours boundaries, and aborts with a `warning: null` if the user does not exist or the ID is missing. When a user record is found, the `getWorkHoursWarning` helper from `@/lib/work-hours` determines whether a warning should be shown based on the user’s workHoursStart/End fields

src/app/api/users/clients/route.ts.description: This file implements a Next.js API route at `/api/users/clients` that returns a list of active client users. The exported GET handler is wrapped in a custom `apiHandler` utility, which standardizes error handling and responses, and includes a `forbidden` helper to reject unauthorized access. Incoming requests must supply an `x-user-role` header that is either `ADMIN` or `PROJECT_MANAGER`; otherwise a 403 response is issued. When authorized, the handler uses the singleton Prisma client to query the `user` table for records where `role` is `"CLIENT"` and `isActive` is `true`, selecting only the `id`, `name`, and `profilePicUrl` fields, and ordering results alphabetically by name. The JSON payload `{ data: clients }` is then returned via `NextResponse.json`. This endpoint is consumed by parts of the UI that display or manage client lists (e.g., an admin dashboard or project‑manager panel), and it relies on the shared `prisma` instance and the authentication/authorization logic in `api-handler`. The `dynamic = "force-dynamic"` export ensures the route is always recomputed on each request, which matches the liability of fetching fresh client data.

src/app/api/users/me/avatar-url/route.ts.description: The route handles authenticated GET requests to /src/app/api/users/me/avatar-url/ and returns the current user’s profile picture URL; it reads the user id from an “x‑user‑id” request header, failing with a 403 if missing. It queries Prisma for the profilePicUrl field of that user, returning a 404 JSON error if the user is absent. If the user has no profile picture, it returns a null payload. When the stored URL begins with the “profile‑pics/” prefix (indicating a Supabase‑stored file), it calls getSignedUrl to produce a temporary secure URL (default 1 hour) and returns that; otherwise it returns the raw URL unchanged. The handler is wrapped by a custom apiHandler middleware that normalises errors and status codes and possibly logs request info. This endpoint is invoked by front‑end components that load a user avatar, enabling the UI to display a signed image for files stored in Supabase without exposing the bucket directly. It depends on @/lib/prisma, @/lib/api-handler, and @/lib/supabase-storage;

src/app/api/users/me/name/route.ts.description: The `src/app/api/users/me/name/route.ts` file implements a force‑dynamic PATCH endpoint at `/api/users/me/name` that allows the authenticated user to update their display name. It first retrieves the current user’s ID from the `x‑user‑id` header, returning a 403 via the `forbidden` helper if missing, and captures the client IP from `x‑forwarded‑for`. The request body is parsed and validated against `NameSchema`, ensuring the name is 2‑50 characters long and

src/app/api/users/me/phone/route.ts.description: The file defines a Next.js route for PATCH `/api/users/me/phone` that updates the authenticated user's phone number. It forces dynamic rendering, extracts the user ID from the `x-user-id` header (or throws a forbidden error), and reads the request body, validating it against a Zod schema that allows nulls or a formatted string of 7–20 characters. After normalizing the phone (trimming or setting to null), it updates the user record via Prisma and selects the updated fields. An audit log entry is created with `logAction`, recording the operation type `PROFILE_PHONE_UPDATED` along with the user's ID, affected record ID, new phone value, and the request IP. Finally, it returns a JSON payload containing the updated phone. This endpoint is used by the profile edit UI to submit phone changes, depends on the shared `apiHandler` for error handling, the `prisma` client for database access, and the `audit` utility for logging; other modules consume the audit log or call this API for user profile updates.

src/app/api/users/me/route.ts.description: The API route `src/app/api/users/me/route.ts` implements the authenticated “current user” endpoint for the Next.js app. It forces a dynamic request and wraps the handler with the custom `apiHandler` to standardize error handling while extracting a user identifier from the `x-user-id` request header, failing with a `forbidden()` helper if missing. The handler queries Prisma for a single user record, selecting a curated set of fields—including nested `notifPreferences`—and returns a 404 JSON response if no match is found. When a user exists and has a `profilePicUrl`, it calls `getSignedUrl` from the Supabase‑storage lib to produce a time‑limited signed URL (3600 s), swallowing any errors and falling back to `null`. Finally, it serializes the fetched user object along with the avatar signed URL as a single `{ data: … }` JSON payload. This route is consumed by front‑end user‑profile UIs that display personal settings and profile pictures

src/app/api/users/me/settings/route.ts.description: The file implements the authenticated PATCH endpoint `/api/users/me/settings`, which updates a user’s account and notification preferences. It extracts the user ID from the `x‑user‑id` header and aborts with `forbidden()` if missing. The request body is parsed and split into parts: user fields (currency preference, client color, work hours) and notification flags, each validated by a partial Z

It first pulls an authenticated user ID from the `x-user-id` header, returning a 403 response if missing. The `x-user-role` header is used to determine how permissive the

src/app/api/users/team-members/route.ts.description: The file defines the GET API endpoint /api/users/team‑members, exporting a Next.js handler that only allows users with role “ADMIN” or “PROJECT_MANAGER” to proceed, otherwise emitting a 403 via the unified `forbidden()` helper from `@/lib/api-handler`. Upon authentication, it queries the Prisma Client for all active users whose role matches any in the internal `TEAM_ROLES` array (PROJECT_MANAGER, DEVELOPER, DESIGNER, HR, ACCOUNTANT, SALES), selecting only id, name, role, and profilePicUrl, and sorting the results alphabetically by name. The `dynamic="force-dynamic"` export forces runtime rendering, ensuring fresh data on each request, which is important for dashboards that display live personnel lists. This API is consumed by front‑end components that display a team roster or populate a dropdown for assigning tasks. The file leverages `@prisma/client` for data access, the shared `prisma` instance in `@/lib/prisma`, and its own role constants to enforce business logic. Other routes that list user data will import the same `prisma` instance and possibly reuse the role-checking logic, making this endpoint a central point for team‑member visibility across the application.

src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/members/[use.description: The DELETE handler in src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/members/[userId]/route.ts removes a user from a workspace, also expunging the user from all related workspace‑group chat rooms and workspace‑task chat rooms and stripping the user from any task assignees. It authenticates the caller via an x‑user‑id header, retrieves the target workspace, and enforces that the caller is either a manager (ADMIN or PROJECT_MANAGER), the workspace creator, or the member themselves for self‑removal, while preventing removal of the last remaining member. If the caller is not a member of the workspace or lacks sufficient role, it returns a forbidden response, otherwise it performs a Prisma transaction that deletes the workspaceMember record, bulk deletes chatRoomMember entries for rooms tied to the workspace or its tasks, and updates each task to filter out the removed user from its assigneeIds array. After the transaction, it logs the removal action via logAction and responds with success. This

src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/members/rout.description: The file implements the POST handler for /src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/members route, enabling a workspace manager to add a new user to a workspace and automatically enrol them in the workspace’s “workspace_group” chat room. The handler pulls the actor’s ID and role from request headers, rejects unauthenticated requests, and validates the target workspace’s existence and that it is active. It confirms that the actor is already a member and has either an ADMIN/PROJECT_MANAGER role or is the workspace creator; otherwise it emits a 403 via the `forbidden()` helper. Duplicate‑member checks return a 409; if the target user does not exist or is inactive a 404 is returned. On success, a Prisma transaction creates a `workspaceMember` row and, if a group room exists, a `chatRoomMember` row, then logs the action with `logAction`. The route depends on `@/lib/prisma` for DB access, `@/lib/api-handler` for wrapper/error utilities, and `@/lib/audit` for logging; it is called by front‑end UI that submits the add‑member form, typically a workspace settings panel. The handler uses Zod for request body validation and forces a dynamic rendering strategy with `export const dynamic =

src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/route.ts.description: The route handler in src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/route.ts implements a protected GET endpoint that returns a workspace’s full details, including members and tasks, only for authenticated users who belong to the workspace. It forces dynamic rendering with `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` and relies on a custom `apiHandler` wrapper that centralizes error handling. The handler extracts a user ID from the `x-user-id` request header, returning a 401 if missing, then queries Prisma for a workspace by `ctx.params.id` that is active, selecting fields such as id, name, type,

src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/tasks/[taskI.description: The `/api/workspaces/[id]/tasks/[taskId]/media` POST endpoint allows authenticated users to upload up to 20 MB files for a specific workspace task, storing the file in Supabase Storage under `workspace-task-media/<taskId>/`; it first verifies the user via the “x‑user‑id” header, then checks membership in the target workspace, and aborts with a 401 or “forbidden” if missing or unauthorized. If the task exists, the request’s multipart form data is read, the file validated, converted to a Buffer, and uploaded using `uploadFile`; the storage path is composed of a timestamp, random suffix, and a sanitized extension. After uploading, the task’s `attachments` array in Prisma is updated to include the new path, and the updated attachments list and any thumbnail path are returned along with a signed URL (1 hour) obtained from `getSignedUrl`. The handler logs the action via `logAction`, enforcing audit compliance, and returns JSON with `path`, `signedUrl`, `attachments`, and `thumbnailPath`. This route is consumed by the front‑end media upload UI for task details, relies on Prisma for relational data, Supabase storage utilities for file handling, and the generic `apiHandler` wrapper for request parsing and error handling.

This file defines the authenticated PATCH and DELETE handlers for the dynamic route `src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/tasks/[taskId]/route.ts`, enabling users to edit or remove workspace tasks from the back‑end. It first extracts the user id and role from request headers, verifies membership with Prisma, and then either updates the task fields (title, description, status, assignees, published date, thumbnail, attachments) or deletes the task entirely. Status changes are gated by `canTransitionWorkspacePost` to enforce role‑based state transitions and auto‑set `postedAt` when publishing. Attachment updates validate that all paths begin with `workspace-task-media/{taskId}/` or are legacy references; removed files are deleted from Supabase storage using `deleteFile`. On every modification, the file logs the action via `logAction`. The delete handler additionally checks author ownership or admin/manager privilege before purging files and the task record. Overall, this route provides the core CRUD API for task objects, relying on Prisma for data persistence, Supabase for media storage, audit logging, and custom business logic for status flows.

src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/tasks/route..description: The POST API at src/app/api/workspaces/[id]/tasks/route.ts creates a new task for a workspace. It first reads an X‑User‑ID header, rejects unauthenticated calls, and verifies the user is a member of the target workspace via prisma.workspaceMember; a missing membership triggers a 403 through the forbidden() helper. The request body is validated with a Zod schema, requiring a title and optional description, status, and assignee IDs (defaulting to “IDEA” status and an empty assignee list). Upon validation, the handler creates a workspaceTask record with the supplied data, specifies creator and timestamps

src/app/api/workspaces/route.ts.description: The file implements the server‐side API for the `/api/workspaces` endpoint, exposing a `GET` list and a `POST` create route wrapped by an internal `apiHandler` wrapper that handles errors and response formatting. A `GET` request first checks for the `x-user-id` header, then queries Prisma for all active workspaces where the user is either the creator or a member, returning each workspace’s id, name, type, description, createdAt, task and member counts, and the first five member records. A `POST` request likewise requires `x-user-id` and additionally ensures the `x-user-role` header is in the `MANAGER_ROLES` set, rejecting others with a 403 response; it then validates the request body against a Zod schema, creates a new workspace record, automatically adds the creator (and any passed member IDs) as workspace members, and in the same transaction creates a corresponding chat room, finally logging the creation action via `logAction`. The module depends on Prisma for database access, the `apiHandler` utility for

src/app/error.tsx.description: This file exports a client‑side React component that serves as an error boundary UI for Next.js’ App Router. When a route or layout throws an exception, Next.js automatically renders the default exported `Error` component, passing it the caught `Error` object and a `reset` function. The component displays a centered, full‑screen card with a headline (“Something went wrong”), the exception’s message, and a “Try again” button that triggers `reset`, causing Next.js to re‑attempt the route rendering. The component is purely presentational, using Tailwind/DaisyUI utility classes, and relies on Next.js to provide the context, with no external imports or side effects. It fits into the codebase as the universal error handler for all nested error boundaries, allowing graceful UI recovery without breaking the rest of the application.

src/app/global-error.tsx.description: This file defines a client‑side component exported as the default from `src/app/global-error.tsx`, intended for use as the global error boundary in a Next.js 13 app where the app router’s `app/` layout calls it on uncaught route or server errors. The component receives an `error: Error` and a `reset: () => void` callback and renders a full‑page HTML skeleton with a dark theme, displaying a “Critical error” header, the error message, and a styled “Try again” button that invokes `reset` to trigger a remount of the page or route. It contains no internal state, side effects, or API calls; all logic is synchronous and purely declarative. The component is automatically wired by Next.js’s error handling system, so any route that throws will show this UI until the user clicks “Try again”, which resets the navigation stack. It depends only on the React runtime (by being a client component), and downstream components—including any pages or layouts that throw—depend on it to provide consistent error presentation across the app. No other files import this component directly; instead, Next.js injects it via the `<ErrorBoundary>` mechanism; key implementation detail is the simple HTML wrapper to ensure markup is valid when rendered outside a layout context.

src/app/page.tsx.description: This file is the root route component of the Next‑JS app‑router; it imports the `redirect` helper from `next/navigation`. When executed on the server, the component immediately redirects any request to the “/dashboard” path, effectively treating the root URL as a convenient shortcut to the main authenticated interface. The file relies on surrounding authentication middleware that has already verified the user’s session before `Home` runs, ensuring that only authenticated requests reach this component while unauthenticated users are directed to `/login` earlier. No UI or state is rendered here—`Home` is purely a declarative redirect trigger, producing a 307 temporary redirect response. Because the component is functionally stateless, nothing else imports or depends on it beyond the routing infrastructure; however, the Next.js router automatically mounts this component for the “/” path based on its file location. This pattern keeps the root route lightweight and delegates all dashboard rendering responsibilities to the `/dashboard` page component.

src/components/ai/AiMarkdown.tsx.description: AiMarkdown is a client‑side React component that renders the raw markdown string of an AI assistant reply; it first removes an outer ```markdown or ``` block to avoid double‑fencing, then feeds the cleaned text to ReactMarkdown with remark‑gfm for GitHub‑flavored syntax, and supplies a custom `components` map that maps Markdown elements to Tailwind/DaisyUI‑styled JSX (e.g., h1 to a bold level‑1 heading, code to a monospace span or block, table to a zebra‑styled table, etc.). The component receives only a `content: string` prop, so it has no internal state, no network or auth side‑effects, and no conditional logic beyond the fence strip; it essentially performs a pure transform from markdown to styled DOM. It lives under `src/components/ai`, depends on the third‑party packages `react‑markdown` and `remark‑gfm`, and is consumed by higher‑level AI chat/message rendering components whose responsibilities include passing the raw reply text to this renderer. This decouples markdown presentation from message fetching or routing, allowing other UI sections (e.g., the AI chat panel) to simply inject formatted text without dealing with styling rules.

src/components/attendance/AttendanceOver.description: The file exports an `AttendanceOvertimeModal` component that displays a modal dialog when a user’s 8‑hour shift ends, presenting two options: start an overtime session (“Work 1 more hour”) or check out for the day. The modal renders a clock icon, a close button, status text, and two styled buttons with Lucide icons, all wired to the `handleAction` function. `handleAction` sets a local `loading` state, sends a POST to `/api
