src/components/attendance/CheckInWidget..description: The `CheckInWidget` is a client‑side React component that displays the current check‑in status and allows authenticated users to check in or out via the `/api/attendance/*` endpoints. It initially fetches status from `/api/attendance/status` on mount and stores it in `status`, updating when check‑in or check‑out actions succeed; `busy` prevents overlapping requests and toggles visual spinners. When the user has not checked in (or already checked out today), it renders a green “Check In” button that triggers a POST to `/api/attendance/check‑in` and shows success or error toasts. If the user is currently checked in, it renders a dropdown showing the clock‑in time and an expected checkout time, with a “Check Out” button that POSTs to `/api/attendance/check‑out`. Toast styling uses custom CSS variables to match the application theme. This widget implements the main UI interaction loop for an attendance dashboard, relies on Tailwind and Lucide icons for visuals, and communicates only with the attendance API; it is likely included in a protected route that guarantees user authentication, but it itself contains no auth logic. It can be reused wherever attendance controls are needed, and any component that displays user presence can import and use `CheckInWidget`.

src/components/auth/FetchInterceptor.tsx.description: FetchInterceptor installs a client‑side wrapper over window.fetch, guaranteeing only one installation and restoring the original fetch on unmount; it runs once in ClientLayout so all authenticated API calls benefit. When a request ends with a 401, it checks that the target is a same‑origin “/api/” endpoint and not the refresh endpoint itself to avoid loops. It then calls POST /api/auth/refresh with credentials, sharing a single promise for concurrent 401s and resetting it after 100 ms. If the

src/components/auth/RoleGuard.tsx.description: RoleGuard is a client‑side React component that protects a subtree of UI by ensuring the current user’s role matches an allowed set. It immediately triggers a GET to `/api/auth/me`, parses the response for `data.role`, and stores that value in a local `role` state. While the role is null, it suspends rendering; once resolved, it checks `role` against the `allowed` array—if the user lacks permission it either renders an optional `fallback` node or uses `router.replace` to redirect to a supplied `redirectTo` path. The component relies on the Next.js routing client hooks and the server‑side `/api/auth/me` endpoint which returns `{ data: { role: string } }`. It is typically wrapped around protected pages or layouts to gate access by role; other components or routes import RoleGuard to enforce role‑based access control throughout the application. The implementation prioritizes a simple fetch‑on‑mount pattern, effect cleanup via an empty dependency array, and conditional rendering tied to user role state.

src/components/auth/ThemeToggle.tsx.description: ThemeToggle is a lightweight client‑side component that exposes a global theme picker positioned at the viewport’s top‑right corner. It consumes a ThemeProvider context, deconstructing the current theme string, a setter function, and the full list of available themes. The component renders a meme‑styled “ghost” button that displays a Sun icon for light themes and a Moon icon for dark‑mode themes, immediately reflecting the active theme. When clicked, the button toggles a dropdown menu listing every theme; each menu item triggers setTheme with its corresponding key and receives an “active” class when it matches the current theme, which also bolds the label. Labels are mapped via a local lookup that translates system keys such as “neutral-dark” into human‑readable titles, with a fallback to the raw key. The component has no side effects other than context updates and relies only on the ThemeProvider and Lucide icon components; it is intended to be imported on any page that requires user‑controlled theme switching, typically shown in authenticated layouts or public sites.

src/components/chat/ChatInfoModal.tsx.description: The ChatInfoModal component renders a modal overlay that displays rich context for a particular chat—images, attachments, links, and recordings—

src/components/chat/MessageContextMenu.t.description: The `MessageContextMenu` component is a client‑side React UI that pops up at user‑specified `(x, y)` coordinates when a message is right‑clicked and offers message‑specific actions. It uses a ref to the menu div, measures its size on mount, and readjusts the position to keep the menu on screen with a

src/components/contracts/ContractUploadM.description: The `ContractModal` component renders a modal dialog that lets authenticated admin upload a PDF contract and assign it to an employee; it shows a user search combo box (unless a `preselectedUserId` is supplied), a title field, and drag‑or‑click file picker that accepts PDFs up to 10 MB. It keeps local state for `title`, `userId`, `file`, `search`, `users`, and loading flags, and initializes `userId` from the prop or via a side‑effect when the modal opens. When opened it fetches the first page of non‑client users from `/api/admin/users?limit=50`; whenever the search query reaches two characters it debounces 300 ms and queries `/api/admin/users?q=…`, updating `users` and a `searching` indicator. On submit, `handleUpload` validates that all fields are present, posts a `FormData` bundle to `/api/contracts`, displays toast notifications on success or failure, triggers the `onSuccess` callback, and then resets and closes the modal. The component depends on the `UserAvatar` UI template for displaying search results and on `react-hot-toast` for feedback; it is typically invoked from higher‑level admin screens such as a contracts dashboard or employee profile. Conditional rendering blocks the `Upload Contract` button until a user is selected, a title entered, and a file chosen, and shows a spinner during the network operation. This file is a pure UI component that orchestrates client‑side data fetching and form handling but relies on existing API endpoints for user retrieval and contract creation.

src/components/dashboard/DashboardCharts.description: The DashboardCharts component renders a four‑panel dashboard inside a 2‑column responsive grid using Recharts: a 6‑month revenue line chart, a task‑status bar chart, a project status pie, and a hiring pipeline pie. It receives a `ChartData` object from its parent, rendering nothing if the data is null, thereby delegating data fetching to higher‑order components such as `Dashboard`. Each chart uses a shared `COLORS` array driven by CSS HSL theme tokens and dark‑mode‑aware border and tooltip styling, ensuring consistency with the host design system (DaisyUI). No side effects or direct network calls occur here; all state is passed down, making it a pure UI renderer. The component depends on the Recharts library and the CSS variable palette, and is imported by the main dashboard page to display analytics after successful authentication. The layout logic (grid, card classes, tooltip configuration) is inline, and no form or search handling is present. This file is a stateless, visually oriented helper that turns pre‑fetched data into rich charts for the authenticated user.

src/components/dashboard/DashboardHeader.description: The DashboardHeader component receives userName and roleLabel props to display a personalized greeting and role indicator in the dashboard’s

src/components/dashboard/DynamicIcon.tsx.description: The DynamicIcon component resolves a string‑based icon identifier into a Lucide icon component for use in the dashboard UI, defaulting to Bell icon when an unknown name is supplied. It defines an ICON_MAP that maps API‑provided names (such as “Inbox”, “Briefcase”, “Target”) to the corresponding Lucide imports. When a consumer renders `<DynamicIcon name="Inbox" className="text-indigo-500" />`, the component picks the mapped icon and applies the provided CSS classes or a default 1.5rem size; the aria‑hidden attribute hides it from screen readers. This file is a pure UI utility, no state or side effects, and is imported by any dashboard feature that needs to display icons based on dynamic data (e.g., task lists, notifications, user profiles). It has no dependencies besides `lucide-react`; any component that receives an icon name from API or state can import `DynamicIcon` and render the appropriate glyph without hardcoding icon imports.

src/components/dashboard/RecentActivity..description: RecentActivity is a read‑only dashboard widget that receives an array of Prisma Notification objects and renders a stylized card listing each notification with a bell icon, title, body, and a human‑readable relative timestamp (e.g., “3m ago”). If a notification includes a linkUrl, the entry becomes a Next.js Link that wraps the content and applies focus and hover styles; otherwise it remains a static div. The formatRelativeTime helper calculates elapsed time in minutes, hours, or days based on the notification’s createdAt field. The component is agnostic of authentication because it simply consumes props passed from a parent (likely the dashboard page or layout) where notifications are fetched from the backend API via Prisma. It imports only the Link module, Lucide icon, and the Notification type, making it lightweight and reusable across any page that wishes to display recent alerts. No internal state or network calls are present; the component relies entirely on the items prop and conditionally renders elements based on linkUrl presence, fitting neatly into a larger dashboard composition that aggregates such widgets.

src/components/dashboard/RoleWidgets.tsx.description: RoleWidgets is a role‑aware dashboard panel component that receives a strongly‑typed DashboardData payload and a UserRole and conditionally renders the appropriate widgets for the logged‑in user. For administrators and project managers it shows a projects grid, a client‑request list, and an approval queue; for developers or designers it lists “My tasks”; for HR it displays the hiring pipeline and today’s attendance alerts; for accountants it presents a ledger entry table; for salespeople it shows deals and tasks, and for clients it shows their projects. The component relies on ProjectCard for project rendering, only performs local rendering logic without additional API calls, and uses the “in” operator to safely access optional fields that may be omitted in the dashboard payload per role. It is mounted by higher‑level dashboard pages that fetch data via server‑side API endpoints, and other parts of the system may import it to provide role‑specific UI within the dashboard route

src/components/dashboard/StatsRow.tsx.description: The StatsRow component renders a responsive grid of KPI for a dashboard, using DaisyUI utility classes and a local icon component (DynamicIcon). It expects an array of DashboardStatItem objects, each containing a label, numeric/string value, an icon name for DynamicIcon, and an optional trend string used for a small status label. The component iterates over the stats array, generating a card with a header row that displays the label in small uppercase text and the icon, a large value text, and conditionally the trend text in a success color. No internal state or side‑effects are present; the component is purely presentational and relies on the icon name mapping supplied by DynamicIcon. Other dashboard pages import StatsRow to show real‑time metrics, while DynamicIcon internally imports icon definitions from a shared icon registry. The file contributes to the visual layout of the authenticated user's dashboard shell and is reused wherever a compact KPI row is needed.

src/components/documents/StandaloneEdito.description: StandaloneEditor is a forward‑ref React component that renders a BlockNote editor with AI‑powered helpers; it exposes a `getContent` method to parent components via `StandaloneEditorHandle`. It accepts optional `initialContent`, `readOnly`, `minHeight`, and an `onChange` callback that receives the current document JSON every time the editor changes. The editor handles file uploads by POSTing to `/api/chat/upload`, then fetching a signed URL from `/api/storage/signed-url` before returning the URL to BlockNote. AI integrations include a “Professionalize” button that triggers `/api/ai/document` with a prompt to improve the selected text, and a modal “Write… ” that invokes the same endpoint to generate and insert content at the cursor, both respecting a 16 k character limit and showing loading spinners; these calls are guarded by `readOnly`. The slash menu is overridden to hide emoji commands and add a custom AI item, and the formatting toolbar is extended with a professionalize icon. The component adapts its theme based on

src/components/layout/Navbar.tsx.description: The `Navbar` component renders a responsive top bar for authenticated users, showing a check‑in widget for certain roles, a notification bell, settings link, and a user profile dropdown; it switches layout between desktop and mobile. The `NotificationBell` manages unread state via a socket namespace "/notifications", polls `/api/notifications/has-unread` every minute, fetches recent notifications on open from `/api/notifications?limit=5`, marks all as read with `/api/notifications/mark-all-read`, and displays a toast with new notification data. The component uses `useSocket` for real‑time updates, `react-hot-toast` for UI feedback, and conditional rendering of a badge when there are unread items. The `SettingsButton` simply links to `/settings`, while `ProfilePill` offers profile viewing, theme switching through `useTheme` from `ThemeProvider`, and logout by POSTing `/api/auth/logout` then redirecting to `/login`. `Navbar` receives the logged‑in `user` prop and a callback `onMenuClick` for mobile sidebar opening. It depends on `UserAvatar`, `CheckInWidget`, and local icon imports, and others such as `next/link`. The file ties into the backend API routes for notifications and auth, integrates theme context, and provides a mobile‑friendly menu trigger, making it central to the authenticated UI shell.

src/components/layout/PresenceProvider.t.description: PresenceProvider is a client‑side React context that tracks the online, away, and offline status of users in the application. It initializes its state by calling the server endpoint `/api/presence` on mount to fetch the current presence map. It then connects to the socket namespace `/presence` via the custom `useSocket` hook and listens for `presence_update` events, updating the presence map by adding or removing user entries according to the received status. The provider exposes this map through `

src/components/layout/Sidebar.tsx.description: The Sidebar component renders a collapsible navigation drawer for the authenticated Dashboard layout, displaying items passed via the `items` prop and handling mobile‑specific open/close state. It tracks the current route with `usePathname` to highlight the active item, expanding nested links when the parent is clicked and collapsing when `collapsed` toggles. Badge counts for items are updated globally via a custom `sidebar-badge` event, stored in the `badges` state, and rendered as small number indicators or error badges. The component conditionally shows a toolbar with collapse/expand and close buttons, and a mobile footer with Settings and Logout options. It uses local icon resolution from a predefined `ICON_MAP` and falls back to a default icon. The component depends on Next.js routing, lucide-react icons, and a sidebar configuration type, and is typically used inside a higher‑level layout that provides auth context and the sidebar item list, while other pages can push badge updates through the event system.

src/components/meetings/MeetingPreJoin.t.description: MeetingPreJoin is a client‑side React component that displays a modal preview of the user’s camera and mic before joining a LiveKit room. It maintains `audioEnabled` and `videoEnabled` state, toggles them via circled icon buttons, and requests a camera stream with `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({video:true,audio:false})` when video is enabled; it releases the stream and clears the video element when disabled or on unmount. The UI shows a live preview when video is on or a muted “Camera is off” message otherwise, and includes a “Join Now” button that calls the supplied `onJoin` callback with the current audio/video flags, and a “Cancel” button that triggers `onCancel`. It relies on lucide‑react icons for mic/video status, and cleans up media tracks to prevent leaks

src/components/meetings/MeetingRecording.description: MeetingRecordingList renders a responsive grid of meeting recording cards fetched from `/api/meetings/recordings` based on optional `meetingId`, `projectId`, `workspaceId`, or `taskId` props, showing title, creation date, uploader, and duration; it shows a centered spinner while loading and a placeholder when none exist. On mount the component builds a query string, calls the API, updates local `recordings` state, and displays error notifications via `react-hot-toast` if the fetch fails. Each card includes a Play button that triggers `handlePlay`, which requests a signed URL from `/api/storage/signed-url`, then opens it in a new tab, handling errors with a toast. The component relies on React hooks, the Lucide icon set for UI visuals, `date-fns` for date formatting, and Tailwind utility classes for styling. It is consumed by higher‑level meeting or task pages to provide users with playable recordings, and its presence implies corresponding backend routes that enforce authentication and query filtering. The component uses no external form state or global context, keeping all data fetching and UI interaction encapsulated within local state, enabling easy reuse across different contexts where recordings need to be displayed.

src/components/projects/AvatarStack.tsx.description: The AvatarStack component renders a horizontally overlapping avatar group for a project or team, optionally showing a "+N" overflow chip when the number of members exceeds the displayed list. It receives an array of AvatarStackUser objects, an overflow count, and an optional presenceMap that maps user IDs to their online status, determining whether to show dotted presence markers and the total online count. For each user it renders a UserAvatar (size 28), layering them with decreasing z‑index and conditionally passing isOnline if the presenceMap indicates "online". When hovered over the stack (using Tailwind group-hover), an absolutely positioned dropdown appears, listing each member with a smaller UserAvatar (size 24), their name truncated, and a presence dot if available, as well as a footer displaying the total number of online users. No network or authentication logic is performed inside this component; it purely acts as a presentational wrapper that depends only on "@/components/ui/UserAvatar" and on the props passed from parent components such as ProjectDetail or TeamCard.

src/components/projects/StatusBadge.tsx.description: StatusBadge.tsx exports a small UI component that renders a DaisyUI badge representing a project's lifecycle state. It imports the ProjectStatus enum from the Prisma client and maps each enum value to a human‑readable label (STATUS_LABEL) and a CSS class (STATUS_CLASS) that defines the badge color. The component receives a single prop, `status`, and outputs a `<span>` with the appropriate class and label text, ensuring the badge is small (`badge-sm`) and does not wrap. It contains no internal state, side effects, or network logic—just deterministic rendering. This badge is typically used wherever a project’s status is displayed, such as in project lists, detail views, or dashboards, and it depends only on the `@prisma/client` type for type safety. No other parts of the codebase alter its behavior beyond passing a valid ProjectStatus; consuming components import it from `src/components/projects/StatusBadge.tsx`.

src/components/providers/ThemeProvider.t.description: The ThemeProvider component supplies a ThemeContext that stores the current theme string (one of the eight predefined values), a setter that writes to the DOM and to localStorage, and the list of available themes; it reads an existing theme on mount and applies it by setting a `data-theme` attribute on the root element and caching the choice, ensuring UI styling persists across sessions. Its `applyTheme` function synchronously updates the <html> attribute, persists to localStorage, and triggers a state update, causing consuming components to re‑render with the new theme, while the effect hook only runs once to hydrate initial state. The provider is intended to wrap the entire application tree, enabling any descendant component to invoke `useTheme` for theme data or to trigger theme changes; it does not perform any network or auth actions. Dependencies are only React and the static THEMES array; other components—for example page layouts, sidebar widgets, or top‑bar settings—must import `ThemeProvider` at root and call `useTheme`. The pattern is a classic context‑value/applied‑attribute approach that keeps theme logic isolated, promotes re‑usability, and ensures CSS custom properties tied to `data-theme` can be used throughout the styling system.

src/components/tasks/TaskKanban.tsx.description: The TaskKanban component renders a draggable Kanban board that splits supplied tasks into five status columns defined in COLUMNS, each column is a KanbanCol that protects its area with @dnd-kit/core useDroppable and visualizes the move state via a dynamic border color. Each task is wrapped in DraggableTaskCard, which uses useDraggable to expose drag handles, shows a grip icon, task title, optional project title, and an AvatarStack of assignees whose current presence status comes from usePresence. Drag start sets the activeTask state; drag end checks the destination column id as the new status, calls the passed onStatusChange async handler if the status actually changed, and clears the active state; the overlay shows a highlighted preview of the moving card. The component expects an onTaskClick callback to handle card selection, but the overlay click handler is intentionally inert. TaskKanban is used wherever a task list needs a column‑based UI (e.g., project detail pages), depending on AvatarStack, PresenceProvider, and DndKit, and is typically orchestrated by parent pages that provide the current authenticated set of tasks and commit status updates to the server via the supplied handler.

src/components/ui/InlineEditField.tsx.description: Resilient component that toggles a single‑field input between read‑only and editing mode, offering inline edits without page reloads. When `isEditing` is true it renders a controlled `<input>` that accepts `text` or `tel` values, wiring `onChange` to propagate changes upward, and uses `onSave` and `onCancel` callbacks plus Enter/Escape key handlers to signal CRUD actions. The UI displays a spinner during `isSaving`, a checkmark to confirm, and an X to abort, with any `error` string shown in small red text. In view mode it shows the current `value` or a dimmed `placeholder` and reveals a pencil icon on hover, styled with DaisyUI classes for consistency across the app. The component has no intrinsic API or auth logic; it merely delegates persistence to the parent component which may perform async updates to a backend. It is intended for reusable inline edit fields such as user profile fields, address components, or settings toggles, and is imported wherever a lightweight edit interaction is needed.

src/components/ui/ResizablePanel.tsx.description: The ResizablePanel component renders a flexible panel whose width can be altered by dragging an edge handle, defaulting to the right side but optionally left‑handed. It initializes width from a persisted localStorage entry specified by storageKey, falling back to the supplied defaultWidth, and clamps the value between minWidth and maxWidth. A mouse‑down listener sets an internal dragging flag, captures start coordinates, and applies a cursor and user‑select style to the body while global listeners track movement; the width is updated with Math.min/max logic and stored in state. When the mouse is released the listeners are removed and styles reset. The component wraps its children in a flex layout that respects min‑w/overflow rules, ensuring the panel content is scrollable without overflowing the visible area. No network or authentication logic is involved; the only side effect is localStorage persistence, making the component suitable for placing within any layout that requires a resizable sidebar, such as dashboards or code editors. It relies only on React hooks and plain DOM events, and other parts of the codebase consume it by providing children and configuration, while it does not export any additional items.

src/components/ui/SkeletonCard.tsx.description: Exports two React components that render placeholder content while data loads. `SkeletonCard` accepts an optional `lines` prop to control how many rows of skeleton bars appear, displaying a pulsing card with a header skeleton and one or more smaller lines; it uses Tailwind CSS utility classes (`card`, `skeleton`, `animate-pulse`) to style the placeholder. `SkeletonCardGrid` composes a responsive grid of these cards, receiving `count` and `cols` props to control how many cards and how many columns at the large breakpoint, mapping each to a `SkeletonCard`. The components contain no local state, side effects, API calls, or authentication logic; they simply output styled JSX. They are grouped under `src/components/ui/`, suggesting they are generic UI helpers, likely imported wherever the app shows loading cards—such as lists, dashboards, or search result placeholders—without being tied to any route or backend. The implementation relies only on CSS classes, so the rest of the codebase can render them as temporary stand‑ins during asynchronous fetches.

src/components/ui/SkeletonTable.tsx.description: SkeletonTable renders a lightweight, fully animated loading skeleton for tabular data, accepting optional rows and cols props to generate a grid of hashed placeholders using Tailwind/DaisyUI classes. The component returns a card container with a pulse animation, an overflow‑handled table, and header and body rows each populated with skeleton divs of varying widths (first column wider, last column narrower, intermediate columns standard). No React state or effects are used; the component is purely presentational and deterministic based on its props. It is typically imported by data‑displaying components (e.g., lists or dashboards) to show a loading state while fetching data via network APIs. The file depends only on global CSS utilities (e.g., "skeleton", "animate-pulse") and has no runtime dependencies; other components that rely on it simply render <SkeletonTable rows={...} cols={...} /> until actual data replaces the skeletons.

src/components/ui/SkeletonText.tsx.description: The SkeletonText component is a lightweight UI helper that renders a configurable number of gray‑blended text lines to serve as loading placeholders for asynchronous content. It accepts an optional `lines` prop to determine how many placeholder rows to show and an optional `className` prop for external styling, defaulting to three lines. Internally it predefines a set of width utilities (`w-full`, `w-5/6`, …) and cycles through them to give each row a slightly different length, creating a more natural text layout. The component outputs a single `<div>` with Tailwind’s `space-y-2` and `animate-pulse` classes, wrapping a child `<div>` for each line that receives the “skeleton h-3 rounded” styles and a width class from the cycle. Because it has no state, side effects, or imports, it is purely presentational and can be imported wherever a loading state needs to mimic text. In the broader codebase, it is typically used in pages or components that fetch data (e.g., lists, detail views) to indicate progress, and other UI modules import it to maintain a consistent loading look without repetitive styling logic.

src/components/ui/UserAvatar.tsx.description: The UserAvatar component renders a circular avatar that displays a user's profile picture if available, otherwise it falls back to colored initials derived from the user name; it accepts a size, presence flag, and online status for optional status dot rendering. It resolves any private Supabase storage path via a signed‑URL fetch from a backend API (`/api/storage/signed-url`) and handles race conditions and errors to avoid showing broken images, while allowing direct HTTP URLs to be used as-is. The component tracks three pieces of state: `imgError` for load failures, `mounted` to avoid client‑only rendering spikes, and `resolvedSrc` for the signed or direct image source. The side effect logic runs on mount and whenever `user.profilePicUrl` changes, decoding Supabase paths, checking for HTTP patterns, and delegating signed URL retrieval. It imports a generic User icon from lucide-react for initial loading and in non‑image fallback cases, and uses Tailwind classes to size text and background colors from a deterministic hash of the user name. This component is a reusable UI building block that other parts of the app (chat lists, member profiles, profile edit screens) import and rely on for consistent avatar rendering, while the signed‑URL API is part of the backend storage service handling access to user-uploaded media.

src/components/workspaces/useWorkspaceSi.description: useWorkspaceSignedUrl is a React hook that, given a relative storage path, asynchronously fetches a short‑lived signed URL from the server’s `/api/storage/signed-url` endpoint, passing credentials so the request is authenticated. It maintains internal state (`url`) that is set to the signed URL or null, resetting to null when no path or on failure, and includes a cancellation guard to avoid state updates after unmount. The hook is used by UI components that need to render or download private files in a workspace context, such as attachment previews, avatar display, or protected document viewers. It relies only on React’s state and effect primitives and on the backend’s signed‑url API; components that import it depend on a valid auth cookie to succeed. The returned URL can be passed to an `` or `` element, or to fetch binary data, enabling secure, time‑bounded access to private storage. This hook is central to any feature that needs to expose protected workspace resources while keeping the storage backend secure and the URLs short‑lived.

src/config/sidebar.ts.description: This file defines the structure and generation logic for navigation sidebars used throughout the web app. It declares a `SidebarItem` interface describing a label, href, icon, optional nested children, back‑button flag, and an optional live‑badge key (e.g., “questionsUnanswered”) that the layout layer updates via a socket subscription. The exported `getSidebarItems` function builds a top‑level sidebar based on the authenticated user’s role string, adding sections such as Projects, Chat, Tasks, Social Media, HR, Finance, Attendance, and Admin only for privileged roles. `getAdminSidebarItems` produces an admin sub‑nav, giving PROJECT_MANAGER full access to Hiring Approvals in addition to Users. `getProjectSidebarItems` constructs a project‑specific sidebar that includes a back link, project dashboard, team‑specific items (AI Chat, Tasks, Meetings) for non‑CLIENT roles, and always shows Chat, Questions (with badge key), Milestone Docs, and Vault. `getHrSidebarItems` returns the dedicated HR sidebar with back to dashboard and hiring, employee management, and contracts sections. These functions are used by the Sidebar component to render the authenticated dashboard shell; the role is typically derived from an auth context, and badge updates are handled client‑side without direct API

src/hooks/useInlineEdit.ts.description: `useInlineEdit` is a reusable React hook that orchestrates the state and lifecycle of an inline‑editable value. It keeps two copies of the data—`value` (the committed value) and `editingValue` (the draft being edited)—and tracks whether the component is in edit mode (`isEditing`), undergoing a save (`isSaving`), and any error message. `startEdit` initializes the draft to the committed value and clears previous errors, while `cancelEdit` exits edit mode without persisting changes. `saveEdit` calls an async `saveFn` supplied by the consumer; if the function resolves with an `{error}` payload it surfaces the error, otherwise it updates `value` and exits editing mode, and any thrown exception is converted to a user‑visible message. `syncValue` lets external state changes (e.g., parent updates or server pushes) overwrite both local copies to keep the UI in sync. UI components such as profile fields, table cells, or form sections can import this hook to provide consistent inline edit semantics without duplicating logic

src/hooks/useSocket.ts.description: The `useSocket` hook establishes and manages a Socket.io connection to a given namespace, caching the socket instance in a module‑level registry so subsequent mounts share the same connection. It stores the socket object and a boolean `connected` in React state, triggering re‑renders when the connection status changes, which is essential for UI components that need to react to socket reachability (e.g., enabling real‑time chat or live updates). In the effect it creates the socket with credentials and websocket or polling transports, registers `connect` and `disconnect` listeners that update the `connected` state, and cleans up listeners on unmount or namespace change, preventing memory leaks. Components such as live dashboards, notification panels, or collaborative editors import `useSocket` to subscribe to specific namespaces (e.g., `/chat`, `/updates`). The hook relies on `socket.io-client` and React’s `useEffect`/`useState`, and other parts of the codebase (like contexts that pass a namespace) use its return values to conditionally fetch data or send messages through the socket. Because the socket is stored in a global `sockets` object, reconnection logic is centralized and only a single socket per namespace is ever instantiated, reducing network overhead and ensuring consistent event handling across the application.

src/lib/admin-user-management.ts.description: The `admin-user-management.ts` module defines a constant `USER_MANAGEMENT_ROLES` listing the roles that are permitted to access user‑management endpoints and `/admin/users`. It exports a helper `requireUserManagement` that inspects an incoming `NextRequest` for the `x‑user‑role` header, returning a 403 JSON response if the role is not one of the allowed values, otherwise returning `null` to allow the request to proceed. This function is intended for use as a lightweight middleware or guard on Next.js API routes and the `/admin/users` page, ensuring that only users with the “ADMIN”, “PROJECT_MANAGER”, or “HR” role can perform actions such as viewing, creating, or editing users. It relies only on the `next/server` API for request/response handling and adds minimal side‑effects, simply validating a header and generating an HTTP 403 if validation fails. Other parts of the application that handle user‑management routes would import and invoke `requireUserManagement` in their route handlers or middleware stack, making this file a central gatekeeper for role‑based access control on admin functions.

src/lib/ai.ts.description: The src/lib/ai.ts module exposes a thin orchestration layer over the Groq chat completions API, exposing three primary helpers: `callAIRaw` returns the raw assistant message (including tool calls), `callAI` returns just the assistant content as a string, and `callAIJson` enforces a JSON output by inserting a strict system prompt that disallows markdown fences or `<think>` tags and attempts a best‑effort JSON parse, falling back to extracting the first JSON payload if parsing fails. It automatically selects a model based on the provided `AITaskType` (e.g., fast, complex, or JSON‑optimized variants) or an explicit override, pulling model names from environment variables `GROQ_MODEL_*` or `AI_MODEL`. The module builds a message array optionally prepending a system prompt and supports function‑style tool calls by mapping `options.tools` into the JSON schema expected by the API and setting `tool_choice`. Network requests are made with `fetch` to `https://api.groq.com/openai/v1/chat/completions`, injecting the Groq API key from `process.env.GROQ_API_KEY`. Errors surface via console logging and return `null` or a fallback failure message. The file is a pure helper with no UI, but is consumed by higher‑level services or components that need language‑model interaction (e.g., chat panels, planning assistants, or data extraction workflows) and can be mocked in tests.

src/lib/api-handler.ts.description: The `api-handler.ts` module exports a higher‑order function `apiHandler` that turns a typed async handler into a Next.js API route handler, automatically catching errors. It normalizes `ZodError` to a 400 response with detailed validation messages, and converts custom error messages “FORBIDDEN” and “NOT_FOUND” into 403 and 404 responses via the helper exports `forbidden` and `notFound`. On unexpected errors it logs diagnostic information only in development, falls back to a generic 500 JSON response, and writes a concise error code for clients. Handlers in the codebase import this wrapper and may throw `forbidden()` or `notFound()` to signal access or data issues; those calls bubble up as specific HTTP status codes. The module depends on `next/server` for request/response types and on `zod` for validation. It is consumed by the back‑end route handlers under `src/app/api/...` where business logic performs database queries or API integrations, ensuring consistent error handling, logging, and JSON payloads across all API endpoints.

src/lib/axios-client.ts.description: Creates a singleton Axios client configured with `withCredentials: true` and attaches a response interceptor to auto‑refresh authentication tokens on 401 responses, queuing subsequent requests until the refresh resolves. When a request fails with 401, the interceptor checks if a token refresh is already in progress; if so, it pushes the request onto a `failedQueue` and returns a promise that will retry the original request once the refresh completes. If no refresh is underway, it marks the request as retried, sets `isRefreshing`, calls the backend `/api/auth/refresh` endpoint, then processes the queue by re‑issuing all queued requests against the refreshed token or rejects them if the refresh fails. A failed refresh triggers a client‑side redirect to `/login`. The module exports this configured Axios instance for all authenticated API calls throughout the application, relying on the global fetch and cookie‑managed session mechanism shared by the backend. This pattern centralizes

src/lib/blocknote/useUIElementPositionin.description: This hook is a Fork of BlockNote's `useUIElementPositioning` that mounts floating UI elements (such as slash‑command

src/lib/dashboard-data.ts.description: The `dashboard-data.ts` file provides a server‑side data loader for the authenticated user’s dashboard that is invoked by the `/api/dashboard` route (or similar). It uses Prisma to query aggregates and lists for each UserRole, applying distinct logic for ADMIN, PROJECT_MANAGER, DEVELOPER, DESIGNER, HR, ACCOUNTANT, SALES, and CLIENT, and returns a consolidated object containing `stats`, `recentActivity`, and role‑specific collections such as `projects`, `myTasks`, `attendanceAlerts`, etc. For ADMIN/PROJECT_MANAGER, it additionally fetches chart metrics (monthly revenue, task/project/hiring counts) in `getChartData`, which is memoized per role and performs a batched month loop. The function computes date ranges per week and month for filtering, uses `Promise.all` to parallelize unrelated aggregates, and falls back to `forbidden()` when the role lacks a defined dashboard. The returned shape is typed as `DashboardData` and consumed by UI components in the client to render cards, tables and charts. The file directly depends on the Prisma client and a generic `forbidden` middleware, and is used by role‑aware pages (e.g., `/dashboard/[role]`) to preload data before rendering the dashboard shell. Key patterns include role‑based early returns, eager loading of nested relations via `include`/`select`, and heavy use of aggregation queries to compute KPI values.

src/lib/env.ts.description: The file configures process environment variables for the Node.js runtime by invoking dotenv’s `config()` to load values from a `.env` file immediately before any other module imports. It then declares a list of mandatory keys—such as `DATABASE_URL`, `REDIS_URL`, token secrets, Supabase credentials, Resend API key, GROQ key, and VAPID keys for push notifications. A loop iterates over these keys and throws a synchronous error if any are undefined, preventing the application from starting with incomplete configuration. No functions or data are exported; the sole side effect is the environment validation that occurs at import time, making this module a lint‑on‑boot guard used across backend services and scheduled jobs. Other modules import it as a dependency to guarantee that all required secrets and endpoints are available before executing any database, cache, or external API logic. This early failure mode also aids DevOps by immediately surfacing missing credentials rather than deferring errors to runtime. The implementation relies only on dotenv and throws plain `Error` objects for straightforward stack traces in deployment and CI pipelines.

src/lib/format-notification-body.tsx.description: The `parseNotificationBody` helper converts a plain notification string into an array of React nodes, enabling rich text rendering within the app’s notification UI. It splits the body on bold (`**text**`) and link (`[text](url)`) patterns, then maps each fragment to either a `<strong>` element with a subtle color scheme, an `<a>` with primary styling and underline, or a plain `<span>` for plain text. The function is side‑effect free, relying only on `ReactNode` types, and is typically called by higher‑level components that render asynchronous notifications or toasts. No state, networking, or auth logic is involved. This utility is imported wherever notification messages need to be displayed, such as in a global notification banner or context‑based toast system, providing consistent styling and linking across the product.

src/lib/notification-icons.ts.description: The `notification-icons.ts` module provides a lookup map that associates each `NotificationType` (a Prisma enum) with a Lucide icon component and a Tailwind CSS color class, enabling consistent visual representation of notifications across the UI. Components that render a notification badge or list simply import `NOTIFICATION_ICONS` and `NOTIFICATION_COLORS` to retrieve the appropriate icon and text color for a given notification, ensuring the badge’s appearance reflects its semantic meaning (e.g., chat messages use an info color, task changes use a warning). The file depends only on `lucide-react` for icons and the type definition from `@prisma/client`, and it is consumed by any front‑

src/lib/prisma.ts.description: The file creates and configures a singleton PrismaClient for the application, ensuring that only one client instance exists across hot‑reloaded serverless functions by storing it on `globalThis`. It first reads `process.env.DATABASE_URL`, optionally adjusting the URL for Supabase hosts by adding a conservative `connection_limit` (≤ 5) and `pool_timeout` if missing, to avoid exceeding Supabase’s pooler limits. The Prisma client is instantiated with the modified datasource URL and logs all queries, errors, and warnings during development while limiting logs to errors in production. The exported `prisma` object is imported by any backend route or service that needs database access, providing a consistent connection layer for CRUD operations. Because it uses `PrismaClient` from `@prisma/client`, it relies on the generated Prisma schema and is typically used in server‑side API handlers or server‑less functions. The global singleton pattern prevents unnecessary reconnections in rapid rebuilds, improving performance and avoiding exceeding Supabase’s connection quotas.

src/lib/project-chat.ts.description: Ensures that every project has two synchronized chat rooms in the database: a “Team Chat” (project_team_group) containing all active project members plus site‑wide admins or project managers, and a “Client & Managers” room (project_client_manager) that includes only the client and the respective admin/managers. The module pulls the current project’s creator,

src/lib/redis.ts.description: The `redis.ts` module centralizes all Redis interactions for the backend: it creates and configures an `IORedis` client, handles password resolution for local versus remote instances, and logs errors at throttled intervals. It exposes helpers for session storage (`setSession`, `getSession`, etc.) that use device‑scoped keys with TTL, a token blacklist API (`blacklistToken`, `markTokenReused`, `getBlacklistEntry`) for detecting JWT re‑use, and presence logic (`setPresence`, `getPresence`, `getAllOnlineUsers`) that tracks online/away status with short expiration to keep the socket layer snappy. The module also implements a sliding‑window rate limiter (`checkRateLimit`) via a sorted set, an OTP attempt counter (`incrementOtpAttempts`) and lockout helper (`blockOtpUser`, `isOtpBlocked`), all of which are intentionally fail‑open to avoid denial of service when Redis is unavailable. These utilities are consumed by authentication routes (token issuance, revocation), WebSocket presence and typing services, and rate‑sensitive API endpoints, making this file a core dependency for auth, presence, and abuse mitigation across the app. Key patterns include using key namespaces (`session:`, `blacklist:`, `presence:

src/lib/request-ip.ts.description: The `request-ip` module supplies a simple, header‑aware source of a client identifier used for rate‑limiting and logging. It exposes two pure utilities: `getClientIp`, which inspects standard forward‑proxy headers (`x-forwarded-for`, `x-real-ip`, `cf-connecting-ip`, `true-client-ip`) and returns the first non‑empty IP, defaulting to `"unknown"`, and `getRateLimitClientKey`, which builds a deterministic key prefixed with `ip:` when an IP is available or falls back to a trimmed `ua:` string derived from the `user-agent` header (sliced to 120 chars). These helpers are invoked in API route handlers or middleware that implement request throttling or per‑client analytics, ensuring consistency across Cloudflare, Cloud Run, and development deployments. The functions depend only on the `NextRequest` type and the request header map, and any component that tracks request origin or enforces per‑client limits imports them. Their logic is straightforward, with no state mutation or network calls, but the header fallback order reflects typical reverse‑proxy setups and prevents leaking private IPs by trimming and limiting the user‑agent string.

src/lib/supabase-storage.ts.description: The `supabase-storage.ts` module creates a privileged Supabase client with the service‑role key and exposes a single bucket (`devrolin-files`) for the application’s media assets. It defines a `StoragePath` union type that restricts stored files to specific folders such as `profile-pics`, `project-pdfs`, `chat-media`, etc., ensuring compile‑time safety for file locations. The `uploadFile` helper uploads a received `Buffer` to the chosen path, overwriting existing content, and throws an informative error if the upload fails; it returns the path on success so calling code can store a reference in a database. `getSignedUrl` generates a time‑limited URL for a stored file, with default 1‑hour expiry, and similarly errors on failure; this URL is used by front‑end routes that need to serve private files without exposing Supabase credentials. `deleteFile` requests removal of a path from the bucket, but silently ignores missing files, allowing idempotent cleanup flows. The module is imported by any API route or server‑side helper that needs to persist

src/lib/tokens.ts.description: tokens.ts centralizes authentication utilities used across the back‑end; it exports constants for access and refresh token lifespans and the secret key, then provides functions to create JWTs, generate opaque refresh tokens, hash them for storage, and verify access tokens. Access tokens are signed with process.env.ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET and expire in 30 minutes, while refresh tokens are 64‑byte random hex strings hashed with SHA‑256 before being persisted. verifyAccessToken safely returns the decoded payload or null if the signature or expiry is invalid. The module also supplies helper utilities such as generateDeviceId (UUID for client identification) and parseUserAgentFamily (converts a user‑agent string into “Mobile”, “Tablet”, or “Desktop” using regex matching). These helpers are pulled into API route handlers, middleware, and token‑refresh logic elsewhere, tying into authentication middleware that protects secure endpoints and refresh‑token endpoints that rotate tokens. The file relies only on the jwt and crypto libraries and environment variables, with all other code depending on it via import to generate or validate tokens and to store hashed refresh tokens in the database.

src/lib/workspace-post-status.ts.description: This file implements role‑based workflow logic for changing the status of a workspace post. It defines the set of all possible `WorkspaceTaskStatus` values and a helper set of manager roles (“ADMIN” and “PROJECT_MANAGER”). The exported functions enforce that managers can move a post arbitrarily, while regular users may only transition among “IDEA”, “IN_PROGRESS”, and “IN_REVIEW”; “APPROVED” can only move to “PUBLISHED”, and “PUBLISHED” or “ARCHIVED” are terminal. `canTransitionWorkspacePost` returns a boolean for any proposed transition and is used by `getAllowedWorkspacePostTargets` to list valid next statuses for a given role and current status. `getPrimaryWorkspacePostTarget` supplies the “happy path” next status (e.g., Idea→In Progress→In Review→Approved→Published) that powers the primary button logic in post‑status UI components. `canArchiveWorkspacePost` simply checks if archiving is permitted. UI icons and button components call `primaryWorkspacePostButtonLabel` and `workspacePostMenuLabel` to render succinct labels like “In review” or “Change to idea”. The module has no side effects; it is pure helper logic consumed by workspace list or detail components when rendering status‑change menus, dropdowns, or action buttons that trigger API calls to update a post’s status on the

src/middleware.ts.description: The middleware intercepts every request matching the global matcher, parsing incoming cookies to extract `access_token` and optionally `refresh_token`; it verifies the access token via `verifyAccessTokenEdge` and, if invalid, attempts a silent refresh with `tryRefreshSession`, updating the cookie header and adding any new `Set-Cookie` headers. Public paths such as `/login`, `/api/auth/*`, and static asset prefixes are allowed to pass unmodified, while a core set of super‑admin routes (`/control`, `/api/super-admin`) are protected by checking that the user role is `SUPER_ADMIN`; otherwise a 404 is returned for API calls or a “not‑found” page for browsers. All other protected routes trigger the auth resolution logic, redirecting unauthenticated browser users to `/login?redirect=…` or returning a 401 JSON for API endpoints. Role‑based permission checks are performed against a hard‑coded `ROUTE_PERMISSIONS` map, redirecting unauthorized users to `/dashboard` or returning a 403 JSON for APIs. The middleware also supports a special guest‑join meeting token scenario by bypassing auth checks when appropriate cookies are missing. It relies on the token verification modules (`@/lib/tokens`/`tokens-edge`) and the refresh helper (`@/lib/middleware-refresh`), and its outcomes (request headers and Set-Cookie lines) are injected into downstream handlers, ensuring authenticated headers reach API routes and that clients receive fresh auth cookies.

src/scripts/bootstrap-users.js.description: The bootstrap-users.js script is a Node‑CLI utility that seeds the relational database with privileged accounts and optional test users. It reads environment variables for SUPER_ADMIN and ADMIN credentials (defaulting to safe‑fallback values), normalizes emails, and validates password lengths before hashing each password with bcrypt and upserting the User record through PrismaClient. The script accepts two flags: --reset, which deletes all Session, NotificationPreference, AuditLog, and User rows to provide a clean slate, and --all, which creates an additional user per remaining UserRole defined in the static TEST_ACCOUNTS array. Each upserted user is followed by creation of a corresponding NotificationPreference row if one does not already exist. When the --all flag is present, the script writes a test-accounts.json file containing JSON credentials for all privileged and test users, warning that this file must be removed before production. The code has no UI or API routes; it is executed manually during development or CI to prepare initial authentication data. It depends on dotenv for configuration, bcryptjs for password hashing, fs and path for file I/O, and Prisma Client for database operations, while nothing else in the codebase calls it directly; it is intended as a standalone bootstrap tool for local or staging environments.

tailwind.config.ts.description: The `tailwind.config.ts` file provides the global styling configuration for the project; it tells Tailwind to scan all JSX/TSX/MDX files under `src` for class names, extends the default theme with no custom tokens, and loads the `daisyui` plugin. Within the `daisyui` configuration it declares a set of custom color palettes (devrolin, neutral-dark, neutral-light, pink, pale) and includes a few preset themes (`light`, `corporate`, `retro`). It designates `neutral-dark` as both the default and dark mode theme, disables DaisyUI logs, and allows other themes to be swapped at runtime through the framework (e.g., via a theme switcher UI). The config is imported by the build tooling (Vite/Webpack) at compile time and has no runtime side‑effects; other parts of the codebase consume its colors through Tailwind utility classes. No APIs, auth logic, or forms are involved—this file simply supplies design tokens and theme switching support for the entire front‑end stack.

src/app/layout.tsx.description: The file implements the root layout for a Next.js 13 app, providing a global HTML structure, meta information, and theme scaffolding used on every route. It declares a `metadata` object containing the page title, description, manifest path, and mobile‑web‑app settings, and a `viewport` object that sets the browser theme color, both of which Next.js injects into every rendered page. The default `RootLayout` component returns an `<html>` element with a neutral‑dark data‑theme, a head that preconnects to Google Fonts and loads the Noto Color Emoji stylesheet, and a body that applies global CSS classes for minimum height, background, text color, and font family. Inside the body the component wraps `children` with a custom `ThemeProvider` that supplies application‑wide theme context, allowing nested components to consume theme state. There are no side‑effects, API calls, or auth logic; its sole responsibility is to supply styling

src/components/layout/ClientLayout.tsx.description: The `ClientLayout` component is the client‑side shell for all authenticated pages; it provides the responsive sidebar, top navbar, AI sidebar, and overtime modal while managing global user context and sidebar overrides. It uses `useSocket("/notifications")` to listen for `new_notification` and `shift_complete` events, showing toast alerts and auto‑opening the overtime modal, and includes a Web Push service‑worker registration and auto‑attendance check‑in for privileged roles on mount. The component stores sidebar collapse state in `localStorage`, toggles mobile overlay and sidebar visibility, and calculates the sidebar width based on collapse status, exposing a `setOverride` function via context to allow children to replace the sidebar item list. The presence provider fetches the initial presence map from `/api/presence` and updates it in real time via a `/presence` socket namespace, while the `FetchInterceptor` attaches authentication tokens to fetch requests globally. Children are rendered within the main content area, and the navbar receives the logged‑in user and triggers the mobile sidebar. Dependencies include `Sidebar`, `Navbar`, `AISidebar`, `AttendanceOvertimeModal`, and various hooks; other components consume `useUser` and `useSidebarOverride` to access user data and modify navigation. This layout is used as a wrapper by Next.js pages under the authenticated dashboard routes.

src/components/meetings/LiveKitMeeting.t.description: LiveKitMeeting.tsx is the primary React component that renders a LiveKit‑based video‑conference screen for authenticated users. It first shows the `MeetingPreJoin` modal where the user previews their camera and mic, and on “Join Now” it connects

src/components/tasks/TaskCard.tsx.description: The TaskCard component renders a clickable card preview of a single task for use in various list or Kanban views; it displays the task title status badge, optional project name, assignee avatar stack, and a small “detail” link adornment, applying dynamic Tailwind classes based on the task’s status. A click propagates to the parent via onClick, enabling card selection or navigation to a task detail page, while a presenceMap prop allows AvatarStack to color avatars for online status. The component relies on lucide icons and AvatarStack, does no network calls itself, but is typically used inside TaskKanban or other task list pages that fetch tasks and handle selection state; the parent supplies `task` data and presence info from a context or provider. The component’s styling includes a subtle hover glow and transition effects. TaskCard is exported for reuse across the tasks module, such as in `src/components/tasks/TaskKanban.tsx` where each card is made draggable, and in pages like project detail or dashboard task lists. It relies only on local props and context, making it pure UI logic that complements the draggable Kanban implementation for task status updates handled elsewhere.

src/components/tasks/TaskDetailModal.tsx.description: The component renders a modal dialog for viewing and editing a single task, displaying project title, status, created date, description, assignees with presence indicators, and an archive button. It owns local state (`task`, `loading`, `saving

src/components/workspaces/WorkspaceKanba.description: The `WorkspaceKanban` component renders a draggable Kanban board for workspace tasks, partitioned into columns defined by `COLUMNS`. It leverages @dnd‑kit to enable dragging cards between columns, updating state via the `onStatusChange` callback when a task’s status changes after a drop. Each column displays a header badge with task count, a button to add a new task (`onAddTask`), and a list of `DraggableCard` components that show task title, creation date, and assignee avatars. Clicking a card triggers the `onTaskClick` callback, while drag interactions modify the `activeTask` state to render an overlay preview. This component receives `tasks` and `members` as props from a parent, likely the workspace detail page; it depends on the local `types.ts` for `WorkspaceTask` and `WorkspaceMember` definitions and exports a single named component for importing elsewhere. Backend interaction is abstracted through the provided callbacks, allowing the calling component to handle persistence, authorization, or navigation logic such as updating a task’s status in GraphQL or REST. The use of `useSensor` with an activation constraint ensures drag accuracy, and the column’s `isOver` indicator visually signals a valid drop target, tying UI cues to drop logic.

src/components/workspaces/WorkspacePostC.description: This component renders a square‑shaped card representing a workspace task within a social‑feed style UI, using the task’s title, description preview, status badge, and assigned members; clicking the card triggers the supplied `onClick` handler. It calculates a media preview path prioritizing thumbnails, image, then video attachments, and leverages the `useWorkspaceSignedUrl` hook to lazily fetch a time‑bound signed URL for secure access to private files, which are rendered as either an image or a muted video overlay; if the signed URL is pending or unavailable a placeholder icon is shown. Descriptions are converted from BlockNote JSON to plain text via `blockNoteJsonToPlainText`. The `AvatarStack` displays up to three assignee avatars, and if a `presenceMap` is given it also shows the count of online assignees. The card’s status badge styles map to the task status constants defined in `STATUS_BADGE`. This component depends on local types, the signed‑URL hook, BlockNote conversion, and AvatarStack; it is likely consumed by a workspace post list or dashboard page to list tasks or micro‑posts. The logic is fully client‑side, with no server

src/lib/ai-context.ts.description: The ai-context.ts module supplies the AI assistant with an authoritative, dynamic memory of a project by creating, retrieving, and updating an internal “__AI_CONTEXT__” document in the database; it uses the global Prisma client to ensure a single connection across serverless reloads. `gatherFullProjectContext` compiles a rich payload that includes project metadata, milestones, member roles, tasks (with optional full details), non‑internal documents (with extracted text if requested), recent chat history, and the current user’s profile, sampling or filtering fields based on supplied flags and the requesting user’s role. `formatContextForAI` then stages this payload into a structured, role‑aware prompt that shows only information a user of a given knowledge level is permitted to see, enforcing the ROLE_KNOWLEDGE_LEVELS hierarchy by selectively masking member details or document previews. `buildChainOfThoughtPrompt` stitches a base prompt with the generated context and a taxonomy of instructions for the LLM to fetch additional data via tools before delivering an answer and optionally updating the context file. The module’s side‑effects are exclusively database CRUDs; it reads and writes only the internal context doc, never directly exposes

src/lib/audit.ts.description: The `logAction` function writes an entry into the `auditLog` table using the global Prisma client, recording the user, action, entity, optional entity ID, metadata, and IP address; it catches and logs errors to the console rather than throwing. It is intended to be called by server‑side API handlers or service functions whenever a notable event occurs—e.g., user authentication, CRUD operations, or workflow steps—to provide traceability and compliance auditing. The optional metadata parameter is coerced to the type declared in Prisma’s schema, allowing arbitrary data to be stored as a JSON column, while the IP address can be supplied by middleware that captures request headers. The function resides in the `src/lib` layer, making it reusable across different routes such as `/api/users`, `/api/orders`, or background jobs that need to write audit entries. Because it relies on the singleton `prisma` client, it benefits from shared connection pooling and environment‑aware configuration (e.g., Supabase connection limits). Existing route handlers import `logAction` and invoke it after successful operations, ensuring consistent audit logging without duplicating database logic. The module thus centralizes audit persistence, decouples logging from business logic, and relies on Prisma’s type‑safe API for safe writes.

src/lib/push.ts.description: The push.ts module configures web‑push VAPID credentials from environment variables and exports a single async routine, sendPushNotification, that delivers a push payload to a registered subscription stored in the database; it first verifies VAPID keys and logs a warning if missing. The routine fetches a user record by id via the global prisma client, extracts the stored pushSubscription JSON string, and parses it into a subscription object for webPush.sendNotification. The payload always contains title, body, the provided url (defaulting to "/dashboard") and static icon/badge URLs for the notification UI. If webPush throws an

src/lib/rate-limiter.ts.description: The `rateLimiter` helper provides per‑client request throttling for Next.js API routes by composing a key from a supplied identifier and the request’s IP or a trimmed user‑agent string extracted via `getRateLimitClientKey`. It delegates to the Redis-backed `checkRateLimit` service, which maintains a sorted set to implement a sliding‑window limiter; based on the returned boolean it either allows the request to proceed (returning `null`) or blocks it with a 429 JSON payload and a `Retry-After` header reflecting the window duration. The function is stateless and purely functional, performing no direct side effects beyond the Redis check and the conditional response construction. It is designed to be invoked inside route handlers or middleware where `NextRequest` is available, ensuring that all API traffic can be uniformly bounded. As a thin wrapper that bundles key generation, Redis cooperation, and HTTP response formulation, it is often imported by authentication endpoints, file‑upload routes, or any high‑frequency API surface to mitigate abuse while keeping the fallback logic fail‑open when Redis is inaccessible.

hocuspocus-server.ts.description: The file launches a Hocuspocus WebSocket server on the port specified by `HOCUSPOCUS_PORT` (3001 by default) and configures it to enable real‑time collaborative editing of documents stored in a Prisma-backed Postgres database. On each client connection, the `onAuthenticate` hook verifies the JWT supplied in the WebSocket handshake using `verifyAccessToken`; it then resolves the document ID encoded in the connection’s `documentName`, fetches the corresponding record, and enforces access control by denying non‑owners of PRIVATE documents. When a document is

src/lib/auth-helpers.ts.description: The auth‑helpers module centralises all cookie‑based authentication side‑effects for the Next.js API by exposing functions that set, clear, and read the access and refresh tokens as well as flow‐specific cookies. `setAuthCookies` writes a persistent, HTTP‑only `refresh_token` and a short‑lived `access_token` to the root path so that the refresh route can always receive the refresh cookie before server‑side rendering occurs, and it purges old path‑scoped refresh cookies from legacy builds. `clearAuthCookies` performs the inverse, expiring both tokens and cleaning the legacy cookie to prevent naming collisions. `setFlowCookie` and `clearFlowCookies` manage one‑time use tokens (e.g., `otp_flow` or `reset_flow`) that enable multi‑step flows such as two‑factor or password reset, with a 15‑minute lifetime. The helper `getCookie` parses the raw cookie header to fetch the last occurrence of a named cookie, favoring last‑defined values when legacy duplicate names exist, and falls back to a parsed cookie map for safety. These utilities are imported by authentication API endpoints (login,

src/lib/tokens-edge.ts.description: tokens-edge.ts supplies an Edge‑runtime JWT verifier for use in the app’s middleware pipeline. It imports jose’s `jwt` and the `AccessTokenPayload` type from the core tokens module, retrieves the binary form of `ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET`, and throws a clear error if the secret is missing. The exported `verifyAccessTokenEdge` function asynchronously decodes a given JWT, returning the typed payload when verification succeeds or `null` when the signature or expiry is invalid, without propagating the underlying exception. This light wrapper is called from `src/middleware.ts` to gate protected routes in the Edge environment, allowing the rest of the code base to rely on a single, consistent verifier. The module depends only on the `jose` library and environment variables, while other components import `verifyAccessTokenEdge` for authentication checks during request handling. Thus, it acts as the fulcrum between the JWT issuance logic in `tokens.ts` and the Edge‑only request validation layer, ensuring token integrity across both API routes and middleware.

src/lib/ai-tools.ts.description: The AI‑tools module exposes a list of OpenAI function‑style tool definitions that the Project AI assistant can invoke and an `executeTool` dispatcher that performs the underlying database queries through the global Prisma client. Each case in the dispatcher fetches data such as project details, members, tasks, documents, questions, recent chat history, or room‑specific messages, applying filters (projectId, status, limits) and ordering to keep payloads reasonable; it also handles updates to the special “__AI_CONTEXT__” document via the imported `updateContextDocument`/`readContextDocument` helpers. The module is therefore the bridge between the LLM “tool calls” emitted during an assistant conversation and the backend

server.ts.description: The server.ts bootstrap file starts by validating all required environment variables (via src/lib/env), then creates a Next.js app instance and an Express server that forwards all requests to the Next request handler. It wraps the HTTP server in a Socket.IO server, storing the instance on a global `io` variable for use in API routes and imports a custom socket-server module to set up namespaces and event handlers. After the HTTP server starts, the script schedules several long‑running background jobs: weekly currency‑rate pulls from Frankfurter, semi‑automatic away‑check pings, hourly auto‑checkout logic with shift‑end and ping expiration rules, nightly absence marking, 5‑minute interview reminders that also push notifications, and per‑minute meeting auto‑end checks for empty rooms. Each job uses Prisma for database operations, Redis for session/timestamp lookup where needed, and sendPushNotification from src/lib/push for real‑time alerts. Errors in any job are logged without crashing the process. The file serves as both the entry point for the Node.js server and the central scheduler for all periodic maintenance tasks, exposing the global Socket.IO instance to other parts of the backend for instant

src/lib/notify.ts.description: The `sendNotification` function orchestrates the creation, persistence, and delivery of user notifications in the application. It first queries the `notificationPreference` table to honour opt‑outs for specific notification types (`CHAT_MESSAGE`, `TASK_ASSIGNED`, `MEETING_SCHEDULED`)

src/lib/chat-send-message.ts.description: Creates and persists a standard chat message in the database via Prisma, setting the server‑received timestamp as “delivered”, and includes the sender, any reply reference, reactions, and receipts for downstream consumption. If a Socket.io server instance is attached to `globalThis.io`, the function broadcasts the new message through the `/chat` namespace to all sockets in the specific room, enabling real‑time UI updates. After storing the message, it queries for all other members of the chat room, obtains the room’s metadata, and constructs a concise preview (first 100 characters or a “Media” tag). It then iterates over each member to trigger a `CHAT_MESSAGE` notification through the centralized `sendNotification` workflow, respecting notification preferences. This module is wired to backend API handlers that handle

src/lib/socket-server.ts.description: The file defines and launches a Socket.io server that powers real‑time chat, presence, and notification features for the DevRolin product. It authenticates every socket connection via a cookie‑based JWT middleware, attaches the decoded userId and role to the socket, and then sets up three namespaced rooms – /​chat, /​presence, /​notifications – each backed by Redis, Prisma, and optional AI logic. In the chat namespace the server lets users join both official room tables and project‑question-only rooms, then handles events for joining/leaving rooms, sending text, media, or promise‑based AI slash commands (e.g., “/professionalize”, “/email”, “@ai”), toggling reactions, marking reads, pinning
